Name: Adeena
Notes: Meet AdeenaTM, the latest release from the ADHA breeding program. Also known as ADHA- 1940, AdeenaTM was initially selected for exceptional vigor, yield, disease resistance, and similarity to Willamette. Following years of expansion, observation, and brewing trials, AdeenaTM has shown herbal and spicy flavor characteristics, with light floral, lemon and pine notes. AdeenaTM has an alpha range between 3.5-5.0%, beta range between 3.0-4.0% and oils ranging from 0.8-1.1. The ~1:1 alpha:beta ratio lends a low, smooth bitterness with a clean finish exceptionally suited for lagers, pilsners, kölsch, and ales. Meaning noble, gentle, and delicate, AdeenaTM is a cross between SummitTM and an English male, has been used as a replacement for Hallertau Mittelfrüh and Styrian Golding. Aroma: herbal, spicy, light floral, lemon, pine notes Typical Analytical Range Co-Hum: 34-38% Total Oil: 0.8-1.1 ml/100g Myrcene: 27.3-29.43% of total oils Caryoph: 14.6-21.9% of total oils Humulene: 31.3-45.4% of total oils Farnesene: 4.3-6.6% of total ADEENA HOP SUBSTITUTIONS Adeena hops carry similar characteristics and flavor profiles as Hallertau Mittlefrüh, Loral, and East Kent Golding making these hops an excellent substitution in your next recipe.

Name: Admiral
Notes: Bittering hops derived from Wye Challenger. Good high-alpha bittering hops. Used for: Ales Aroma: Primarily for bittering Substitutes: Target, Northdown, Challenger

Name: African Queen
Notes: A high alpha hop from South Africa with interesting bittering potential and fruity flavors. Fruity aroma includes hints of blueberry, cassis, passion fruit, citrus, stone fruit, black currant, tea, lemongrass and even chili. Can be used both for bittering and as a late addition.

Name: Aged Hop Blend
Notes: From Yakima Chief Hops - a blend of aged hops. Aroma Profile: Dried Fruit, Hay, Herbal, Woody

Name: Agnus
Notes: Several large Czech breweries like Agnus as a bittering hop because its alpha/beta ratio is relatively high, which they believe makes their beer more stable. High geraniol level, oils indicate dry hopping potential. Used for: Ales Aroma: Strong hop aroma, spicy Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 9-15% AA / 4-8% Beta

Name: Ahhhroma Hops
Notes: Ahhhroma was a serendipitous discovery from Glacier Hops Ranch's original research hopyard, where a cross-bred seed emerged as a mix of the up to 44 different hop varieties that have been grown there. This mysterious origin story adds a layer of intrigue to the hop, which has been cultivated and pursued over the years for its surprising and valuable characteristics. With an alpha acid percentage that ranges from 15% to 19%, Ahhhroma stands out as a super-alpha bittering hop, capable of delivering a robust bitterness that is essential for many beer styles. However, the true allure of Ahhhroma lies in its aromatic profile, which is rich in oils and presents a bold tropical fruit character. Brewers will appreciate the hop's versatility, as it can impart a nuanced flavor profile reminiscent of a Piña Colada, bursting with notes of pineapple, pear, mango, and lime. The hop carries secondary notes of stone fruit, watermelon, and even subtle spices like cinnamon and nutmeg. It is especially suitable for heavily hopped beer styles such as India Pale Ales, including the traditional, New England, Hazy, and Imperial varieties, as well as Pale Ales and India Pale Lagers. Flavor/Aroma: Cinnamon, Lime, Mango, Nutmeg, Pear, Stone Fruit, Pineapple, Tropical Fruits

Name: Ahtanum
Notes: Distinctive aromatic hops with moderate bittering power from Washington. Used for: American ales and lagers Aroma: Distinctive floral and citrus aromas Substitutes: Amarillo, Cascade

Name: Aloha
Notes: The Aloha hop is a relatively lesser-known variety in the world of brewing. Although it has not gained widespread popularity, the Aloha hop offers an intriguing flavor profile that can inspire unique and exciting beer creations for adventurous homebrewers. Characterized by a delightful blend of berry, citrus, floral, and fruity notes, the Aloha hop lends itself perfectly to crafting beers with a refreshing and vibrant taste. This versatile hop can be utilized in various beer styles, including Pale Ales, IPAs, and even fruited sours. When using Aloha hops in your homebrew, consider incorporating them in late boil additions or dry hopping to maximize their aromatic and flavorful potential. As a homebrewer, experimenting with the lesser-known Aloha hop variety can lead to the discovery of new and exciting flavor combinations, resulting in a truly unique tasting experience. Flavor/Aroma: Berries, Citrus, Floral, Fruity, Sweet

Name: Alora
Notes: New aroma variety with unique oil chemistry and high bound 3MH thiols Aroma: Peach, Yuzu Fruit, Sweet Melon, apricot Alpha Acid: 7.0 - 10.0% Beta Acid: 3.5 - 4.0% Total Oil: 0.8 - 1.3 ml/100g

Name: Altus
Notes: A high alpha hop packed with spicy, dank, and resinous aromas that lend to soft tangerine and herbal notes in finished beer. Aroma: Spicy - resinous, tangerine, citrusy, herbal, dank, grassy Alpha Acid: 13.5 - 16.5% Beta Acid: 3.5 - 5.0% Total Oil: 3.0 - 4.0 ml/100g

Name: Amallia
Notes: A native plant from New Mexico and part of the neomexicana varietal. Trialed by Michigan State University in 2014. A deeply earthy aroma with flavors of orange citrus. Well suited for brown or dark ales.

Name: Amarillo
Notes: Discovered and introduced by Vigil Gamache Farms Inc. in Washington State. Character similar to Cascade. Often used as a late kettle or dry hop addition to American style Pale Ales and IPA’s due to its signature aroma characteristics. Used for both bitterness and aroma. Used for: IPAs, Ales Aroma: Intensely fruity (citrus, melon, and stone friuts), floral, tropical notes. Substitutes: Cascade, Centennial, Simcoe Storage: Good 8-11% AA / 6-7% Beta

Name: Amarillo GR VGXP01
Notes: Discovered by Virgil Gamache Farms, Amarillo® Brand VGXP01 is one of the top ten aroma varieties utilized by craft brewers. It features relatively high alpha acids along with extremely high levels of myrcene for citrus and grapefruit flavors. Aroma Profile: Citrus, Herbal, Spicy, Stone Fruit, Woody

Name: Amarillo Gold
Notes: Unknown origin, but character similar to Cascade. Used for both bitterness and aroma. Used for: IPAs, Ales Aroma: Citrus, flowery Substitutes: Cascade, Centennial

Name: Amethyst
Notes: Low alpha aroma hops related to saaz grown in the Czech Republic. Citrusy, earthly and spicy. Substitutes: Saaz

Name: Amira
Notes: Breeding/Development: From the HVG Hop Breeding Program, Amira boasts genetics from wild and domestic hops from Germany, Russia, the UK, and the US, including Brewers Gold. In the field it is heat- and drought-stable with vigorous growth and high yields. Sensory: Classic hoppy, slightly herbal aroma with hints of citrus (lemon). Spicy and sweet fruit undertones. Early sensory feedback compared Amira to Perle and Hallertau Tradition. Brewing Application: Aroma and finishing additions. Amira works well as a solo hop or part of a blend in traditional German beer styles as well as craft lagers: use it for Pilsner, Vienna, Kolsch, Altbier, Bock, and more.

Name: Amora Preta
Notes: The Amora Preta hop is an intriguing fusion of potency and flavor, marked by a robust alpha acid content and a captivatingly intense fruity aroma. This hop variety intertwines tropical allure with the essence of Polish forest fruits, most notably blackberries, while harmonizing with complimentary resins and zest (mandarin, oranges, red grapefruit, lime). Amora Preta stands as the epitome of excellence for Single Hop beers. Flavor: Amora Preta unveils a tapestry of flavor where potency meets finesse. The bold alpha acid content is complemented by a captivating array of tropical notes, seamlessly blending with the essence of Polish forest fruits, notably blackberries. This hop transforms a brew into an exquisite dance of strength and subtlety. Aroma: Immerse your senses in the intoxicating aroma of Amora Preta. The richness of blackberries takes center stage, joined by tropical allure and underscored by resin and zest. Mandarin, oranges, red grapefruit, and lime intertwine, adding layers of complexity that elevate the aromatic symphony to new heights.

Name: Anchovy
Notes: Anchovy is a new experimental variety from Segal Ranch in the lower Yakima Valley. Initially, it went by the name 24B-05 but was given the controversial name Anchovy by Matt Storm and Brian Strumke from Fast Fashion Brewing in Seattle. Fast Fashion was the first brewery to sponsor acreage, so they have been the only folks to get a chance to brew with it so far, but now there is a very limited amount of 2023 crop year available. Fast Fashion Brewing describes the variety as "watermelon hard candy, raspberry, and pine." Try it for yourself today and brew up something amazing!

Name: Apollo
Notes: Super high alpha variety from the Hopsteiner breeding program released in 2006. High alpha and low cohumulone makes it a great bittering hop. Adds a strong grapefruit/hop note if added late in boil. Used for: IPA, IIPA Aroma: Strong grapefruit and hoppy notes Substitutes: Nugget, CTZ, Bravo Storage: Excellent/Very Good (80-90% AA remaining after 6 months at 20 C) 15-19% AA / 5.5-8% Beta

Name: Apolon
Notes: A Slovenian hybrid that is a cross between Brewer's Gold and a Yugoslavian wild male. Once thought to be related to Super Styrian. Traditionally bred for bittering can also be used as an aroma hop.

Name: Aquila
Notes: Aroma hops developed in 1988. Limited use due to high cohumolone. Used for: Aroma hops Substitutes: Cluster No longer commercially grown.

Name: Aramis
Notes: Very fine spice and herbal notes with subtle citrus characteristics. Closely resembling Strisselspalt, but with higher oil and alpha content. Used for: Belgian-style Ales Substitutions: Willamette, Challenger, Ahtanum Storage: Unknown

Name: Arcadian
Notes: Arcadian hops, cultivated in the United States of America, are a product of Great Lake Hops. This hop variety is primarily used for its aromatic qualities, especially in late boil additions and dry hopping. The flavor and aroma profile of Arcadian hops are distinct and captivating. As noted from the SMaSH brew sensory conducted by the GLH team, Arcadian hops impart unmistakable orange/tangerine flavors and aromas to the beer. This aligns with the sensory notes that highlight its light essence and floral nature. Aroma: Floral, Orange, Tangerine

Name: Archer
Notes: A new variety from the Charles Faram Hop program. Classic British aroma with a citrus twist. Aroma is floral with delicate hints of lime and peach. Provides traditional British assertiveness.

Name: Ariana
Notes: Developed at the hop research center in Hull, Germany. A cross between Hurkules and a wild male. Aromas include black currant, grapefruit, gooseberry, geranium and vanilla.

Name: Astra
Notes: Astra hops have sweet tropical fruit flavors and New World characteristics. Flavor profiles with green melon, white peaches, white wine and a light grassiness, grapefruit and honey, the hop balances out nicely between a sweetness with a bit of a bite. Aroma: tropical_fruit, sweet. peach, white_wine, melon, grassy. grapefruit. honey

Name: Atlas
Notes: A cross between Brewers Gold and Slovenian wild hops. Primarily a bittering hop with pleasant bitterness and aroma. Used for: Bittering ales, lagers Substitutes: Brewers Gold

Name: Atlas (Styrian Atlas)
Notes: A cross between Brewers Gold and Slovenian wild hops. Primarily a bittering hop with pleasant bitterness and aroma. Used for: Bittering ales, lagers Aroma: Fresh hoppy aroma Substitutes: Brewers Gold Storage: Very Good 7-11% AA / 3.2-41% Beta

Name: Aurora
Notes: Also called Super Styrian. This is a hybrid between Northern Brewer and TG. It has a pleasant hoppy aroma similar to Styrian Goldings but lighter. Used for: Often mixed with other hops in lagers. Substitutes: Styrian Goldings

Name: Aurora (Super Styrian Aurora)
Notes: Also called Super Styrian Aurora. This is a hybrid between Northern Brewer and TG. Pleasant hoppy aroma similar to Styrian Golding but lighter. Used for: Strong Ale, Pale Ale, Bitter Aroma: Intense and pleasant hoppy aroma Substitutes: Styrian Golding Storage: Excellent/Very Good (80-85% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 7-9% AA/ 3-5% Beta

Name: Azacca
Notes: Notes: Dual purpose hop. Good for dry hopping Aroma: Citrus, tropical, floral

Name: Aztec
Notes: Aztec™ has risen from the ashes as the most unique among her Neomexicana sisters. Boasting superior growth, disease resistance and an incredibly pleasant aroma profile. Aroma: Lemon, Pepper, Herbal, Spice Cilantro, Lime zest, Lemon Candy, Jalapeno Licensed by Great Lakes Hops.

Name: Banner
Notes: Bittering hops developed in 1988. Use largely discontinued due to poor storage. Used for: U.S. style ales Substitutes: Cluster

Name: Barbe Rouge
Notes: Barbe Rouge is a new and exciting hop variety developed in the legendary growing region of Alsace, France. Barbe Rouge is a bright and delicate aroma hop that offers ripe red fruit flavors of currant, strawberry, and raspberry. It also offers zesty citrus aromas of oranges and limes while some people describe a unique kumquat character. Barbe Rouge is well suited for American IPAs, NEIPAs, and pale ales while still being delicate enough to use in a modern twist on a classic pilsner. Alpha Acid: 7 - 10% Total Oil 1.1-1.6ml/100g Myrcene 35-52 % Humulene 15-25 % Humulene / Caryophyllene 2.5-2-8 Linalool 4-5mg/100g Farnesene 2.5-3.5 mg/100g Geraniol 10 - 15mg / 100g

Name: Beata Hops
Notes: Beata is a low alpha British aromatic hop developed at Wye College in the UK in 1995 though it did not go into production until 2006. Lovely aroma, with classic British complexity. High beta levels also contribute unique properties. Honey, apricot and almond notes.

Name: Belma
Notes: Belma® hops are a recent addition from Hops Direct and Puterbaugh Farms in the Yakima Valley. Belma has been tested as a dual-use hop and found to have an ambrosial mix of orange, melon, strawberry and pineapple with a slight hint of grapefruit. Backing that up, OSU created a Pale Malt with Belma which carried those same aroma and flavor characteristics. To date, Belma has also seen plenty of activity in the recreational brewing space with many home brewers documenting its use online in Pale Ales, IPAs, Blonde Ales and more. Virtually all have given it favorable marks. It was released in 2012.

Name: Bergamot
Notes: ​​Bergamot™ subtly nicknamed "O.A.F." by the brewers in the GLH Brew Group that have crafted incredible first round trial brews with this hop. There is no lack of orange in this hop. You won't need to garnish your beer with an orange slice ever again. Licensed by Great Lakes Hops Aroma: Orange, Dank, Cherry, Zest, Lemongrass, Lime Aid, Juniper, Orange Juice, Mango

Name: Bertwell
Notes: Bertwell hops have a fascinating origin story. They were discovered growing wild near Picton, Ontario, and subsequent test brews and laboratory studies have led to the belief that this variety may be a survivor from Ontario’s original pioneer brewing days, when Upper Canada was a barley belt for the British Empire. The hops were named Bertwell in honor of Bert Grant, a renowned craft brewer and hop lover, and Wellington County, the site of their first commercial hopyard. It's thought that these plants are probably British survivors from Prince Edward County’s 'Barley Days', a period when Upper Canada was a barley and hop growing stronghold of the British Empire. Flavor: Citrus, Floral

Name: Bitter Gold
Notes: Intense stone fruit especially pear and watermellon. A super-alpha variety with excellent aroma. Used for: All Styles Substitutions: Galena, Nugget

Name: Blato
Notes: Blato was one of the first hops authorized in Czechia (formerly Czechoslovakia) for commercial production. It is not usually singled out, but is one of the collective known as Saaz Hops, or Bohemian Early Red. It doesn’t yield very well in the U.S., but it is suspected to be much more prolific in its native homeland. Zatec Hop Company in Czechia states that in the analysis of the oils and resins, Blato and its siblings have proven to have the same fine aromatic profile of other Saaz hops one that is genuine and delicate. Flavor/Aroma: Earthy, Floral, Herbal, Mild, Noble

Name: Boadicea
Notes: Released in 2004. Excellent for finishing and dry hopping. Used for: Ales Aroma: Mild floral and spicy characteristics with slightly grassy overtones Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 7-10% AA / 3-4% Beta

Name: Bobek
Notes: Also called Styrian Golding B - a cross between Northern Brewer and a TG seedling. Pleasant hop aroma and flavor. Used for: Moderate English ales and lagers/Pilsners Substitutes: Northern Brewer

Name: Bobek (Styrian Golding B)
Notes: Also called Styrian Golding B - a cross between Northern Brewer and a TG seedling. Used for: English Ales, ESB, Lagers, Pilsners Aroma: Intense and pleasant hoppy aroma Substitutes: Northern Brewer, Styrian Golding, UK or US Fuggle, Willamette Storage: Unknown 3.5-7% AA / 4-6.1% Beta

Name: Boomerang
Notes: Released in 2017, this hop variety from the Czech Republic has quickly garnered attention for its unique aroma and flavor profile. Boomerang hops are known for their spicy and hoppy characteristics, complemented by a slightly citrussy undertone. Additionally, they carry notes of fruity aroma, making them a versatile choice for brewers. These hops are particularly suitable for lagers and ales, two beer styles that can benefit from the distinct flavors that Boomerang hops bring to the table. Boomerang hops are primarily used as a bitter hop variety. They are ideal for both first and second hopping, ensuring that the beer has a robust and layered hop profile. Furthermore, for those looking to craft special beers with a unique twist, Boomerang hops can be used for dry hopping. This method enhances the aroma and flavor of the beer, giving it a fresh and vibrant character that stands out. Characteristics

Name: Bouclier
Notes: A cross between Alsace grown Strisselspalt and a wild male plant from Wye, Kent. Grassy with hints of spice. Aromas of herbs, grass, spice with some floral and citrus character.

Name: Bramling
Notes: Bramling hops, popular in England around the turn of the 20th century, were extensively used in golden and rye ales. Despite their origin being unknown, they were widely cultivated by 1865. However, due to their increasingly low yield, they have fallen out of favor over time. Today, they are grown in British Columbia, catering to Canadian breweries that desire aroma hops. Bramling hops are characterized by their very low alpha acids, around 5.8%, and moderately low cohumulone, approximately 27%. This combination results in a pleasant European aroma, making them an ideal choice for beers where a more pronounced aroma is desired. Their flavor profile is quite distinctive, with notes of lemon, blackcurrant, blackberries, and plums, especially when used as a late addition in the brewing process. These hops are particularly suited to all English beer styles, including golden ale, bitter, rye ale, India pale ale, stout, and holiday ale. Flavor/Aroma: Black Currant, Blackberry, Lemon, Plum

Name: Bramling Cross
Notes: Developed in 1927 from commercial Goldings and wild hop variety. Used for: ESB, Bitters, Pale Ale Aroma: Mild, fruity, currant aroma. Substitutes: East Kent Goldings, Progress, Whitbred

Name: Bravo
Notes: A second generation super high alpha hops from the Hopsteiner breeding program released in 2006. Good bittering hop. Aroma: Fruity and floral aroma. Substitutes: Columbus, Zeus, Tomahawk

Name: Brewer's Gold
Notes: Primarily a bittering hop and commonly used in lighter style ales, although it works well in lagers. Used for: English ales, German styles, farmhouse ales. Aroma: Bittering hop with neutral aroma character, spice noted to its flavor and aroma Substitutes: Northdown, Northern Brewer (Germany), Galena, Bullion, Chinnok, Cascade (U.S.) Storage: Fair (60-70% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 7-10% AA / 3.5-4.5% Beta

Name: Brewer's Gold, Germany
Notes: Black currant, fruit and spice characteristics. Has a high resin content and gives a well-balanced bitterness which is ideally complimented by late hop additions in lager beers. Usage: Ale, Bière de Garde, Lambic, Lager, Saison Substitutions: Galena, Northdown, Northern Brewer Storage: Unknown

Name: Brewer's Gold, UK
Notes: Brewers Gold is a complex bittering hop that is noted to have a sharp or pungent bittering quality. It also imparts a fruity yet spicy aroma as well as having a black currant characteristic. It is on the low end of the bittering hop scale at 8 to 10% AAU typically. Brewers Gold can be used in a wide range of styles from English Ales to German Lagers and adds a decidely 'European' element to the beer. These make a good partner to noble varieties such as Tettnang and Hallertauer. Used for: English Ale, German Lagers Substitutions: Bullion, Chinook, Galena

Name: Brooklyn
Notes: A high alpha hop with distinctive tropical, grapefruit and passion fruit characters. Developed from Southern Cross. Used for: IPAs Substitutions: N/A

Name: Bullion
Notes: A major hop variety since the mid-1940s, however, product ceased in 1985 due to super alpha hops with better storage stability. Commonly used in darker beers. Used for: Stouts, ESB, Scotch Ale, IPA, Porter, Heavy German lagers. Aroma: Strong, pungent and bitter in flavor. Intense Black currant aroma. Substitutes: Chinook, Galena, Eroica, Columbus, Northern Brewer (German), Brewer's Gold (German). Storage: Poor (40%-50% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 6.5-9% AA / 3.2-6% Beta

Name: Bullion
Notes: Used in strong dark English ales, some heavy German lagers. Aroma: Strong, pungent and bitter in flavor. Blackcurrant flavor. Substitutes: Chinook, Eroica, Brewer's Gold

Name: Bullion 10A
Notes: Though technically still Bullion, Bullion 10A is a heat-treated and meristem-tip cultured version of the original English variety. Developed by the USDA and released in 1972, it was retired from production in the US in 1985 along with the original Bullion following the introduction of super-alpha varieties.

Name: CTZ
Notes: CTZ is a trio blend of similar hops including Columbus, Tomahawk and Zeus. A descendant of Nugget, it is the first super high alpha variety and is primarily used for bittering purposes. Aroma Profile: Citrus, Dank, Earthy, Woody

Name: CTZ Hop Hash
Notes: Technically this is lupulin powder similar to Cryo-hops. It is intended for use primarily in the whirlpool, hot steep or for dry hopping. Columbus, Tomahawk and Zeus are collectively called CTZ and are similar in character and often blended. They provide citrus aromas, black peper, licorace and curry notes.

Name: Calicross
Notes: New Zealand Calicross was developed in the 1960's and largely disappeared in the 1980s. Can still occasionally be found. A cross between Fuggle and Late Cluster. Earthy, fruity flavors with floral aromas. Substitute: Cluster

Name: Calypso
Notes: A new, dual-purpose hop from the Hopsteiner breeding program that may establish itself as "special". Used for: Ales, Stouts and Barley Wines Aroma: Not as tropical as the name might imply but rich in both stone fruits (pear and peaches, notes of cherry) and citrus. Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Good (65-70% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 12-14% AA / 5-6% Beta

Name: Canadian Redvine
Notes: From Canada and very disease resistant. It has a very high cohumulone content and low alpha making it a good late hop candidate. Aroma and flavor of berries, pine, cherry and grapefruit peel.

Name: Cascade
Notes: A hops with Northern Brewers Heritage Used for: American ales and lagers Aroma: Strong spicy, floral, grapefruit character Substitutes: Centennial Examples: Sierra Nevada Pale Ale, Anchor Liberty Ale

Name: Cascade Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Made from Cascade hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: Cascade, Argentina
Notes: Unlike the American Cascade, the Argentine Cascade has a mellow character with notes of lemongrass, pepper and spice. It is not considered interchangeable with the American Cascade, which has a very different profile. It is a fine aroma and finishing hop, excellent for wheat beers, blondes and has been suggested as being especially suited to lightly bittering a cream ale. Argentinian Cascade is often compared to Hallertau or Tettnanger.

Name: Cascade, New Zealand
Notes: Flowers, citrus & spice with grapefruit the noticeable fragrance quite often. This medium aroma balances the low bittering value. Very popular hop among craft brewers. Used for: Pale Ales, IPAs, Porters Substitutions: Centennial, Amarillo

Name: Cashmere
Notes: Daughter of Cascade. Mild herbal aroma with strong melon, lemon, and slightly spicy notes. Used for: American Pale Ale, IPA Substitutions: N/A

Name: Cekin
Notes: Developed at the Hop Research Inst in Slovnia in the 1980's. A cross between Aurora and a Yugoslav male. Not grown in large quantities. Pleasing and continental aroma similar to Savinjski Golding. Substitute: Savinjski Golding or Aurora

Name: Celeia
Notes: A dual purpose hops that is a hybrid between Savinjski Golding, Aurora and Slovenian wild hops. Similar in aroma to Saaz. Used for: Light lagers, Pilsners, Belgian ales. Substitutes: Saaz, Styrian Goldings

Name: Centennial
Notes: Used for: General purpose bittering, aroma in American ales and Wheats Aroma: Floral, citrus aroma, spicy, clean bittering flavor Substitutes: Galena, Eroica, Nugget, Bullion Examples: Sierra Nevada Celebration Ale, Sierra Nevada Bigfoot

Name: Centennial Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Extracted from Centennial hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: Centennial Hop Hash
Notes: Technically this is lupulin powder similar to Cryo-hops. It is intended for use primarily in the whirlpool, hot steep or for dry hopping. Columbus, Tomahawk and Zeus are collectively called CTZ and are similar in character and often blended. They provide citrus aromas, black peper, licorace and curry notes.

Name: Challenger
Notes: Variant of Northern Brewers heritage Used for: Both aroma and bittering for ales and lagers Aroma: Fruity in flavor, spicy aroma, blends well Substitutes: N/A

Name: Chelan
Notes: High alpha variety from the Haas breeding program released in 2004. It is a variant of Galena and very similar in profile to Galena. Aroma: Pleasant citrus notes Substitutes: Galena

Name: Chinook
Notes: Strong versatile bittering hop Used for: Ales, porters, stouts Aroma: Heavy and spicy aroma Substitutes: Galena, Eroica, Nugget, Bullion Examples: Sierra Nevada Stout, Sierra Nevada Celebration Ale

Name: Chinook Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Extracted from Centennial hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: Chinook, New Zealand
Notes: Piney with exotic spice and infused with grapefruit. This alluring aroma and a high bittering value has gained this hop full respect from craft & major brewers. Used for: American Pale Ale, IPA, Porter, Stout Substitutions: Southern Cross, Sticklebract, Nugget, Northern Brewer

Name: Cicero
Notes: Also known as HBC 394. Sister to Cekin, Cicero is a dual use hop with a Styrian-type aroma bred from Aurora, a Yugoslav tetraploid male and a USDA tetraploid male. Developed in Slovenia in the 1980’s, Cicero possesses very high vigor and yield potential in home region but has not tested well in the US.

Name: Citiva (Sitiva)
Notes: Added to the BSG Hop Solutions portfolio in 2020, Sitiva was developed through multiple rounds of brewing trials at the Rahr Technical Center pilot brewery. It is a blend formulated specifically for use in hazy IPA and other hop-forward beers. Citiva hops are known for their strong expression of ripe stone fruit, sweet floral and soft peach flavors, and aromas, as well as tropical fruit and lime character. They are best used for aroma additions, whirlpooling, and dry hop additions in brewing. Flavor/Aroma: Sitiva has ripe stone fruit, sweet floral, and soft peach flavors and aromas, as well as tropical fruit and lime character.

Name: Citra
Notes: Special aroma hops released in 2007. Imparts high alpha/oil content but low cohumulone. Aroma: Adds interesting citrus and tropical fruit character to the beer. Substitutes: Unknown

Name: Citra Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Extracted from Citra hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: Cleopatra
Notes: Cleopatra hops are a relatively new addition to the American hop scene. Originally known as G1, Cleopatra boasts a delightful flavor profile, characterized by pleasant citrus notes complemented by a light pine and resinous touch. Intriguingly, it also exhibits some tropical nuances and hints of German spicy tendencies. While it doesn't possess the same oil content as the renowned Citra hop, its profile aligns closely with the Cascade variety. Homebrewers, particularly those who have had the pleasure of brewing with Cleopatra, often find it to be a standout. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Pine, Resinous, Tropical Fruits

Name: Cluster
Notes: Used for: General purpose bittering hop Aroma: Floral, slightly spicy. Sharp aroma and sharp flavor Substitutes: Eroica, Galena

Name: Cluster Fugget Blend
Notes: Cluster Fugget™ is a spontaneous hop blend that is just that...a cluster Fugget of Pacific Northwestern varieties, capturing the adventurous spirit of the home brewing community. Whether it is your first batch, or your one-hundredth, every home brewer has wound up brewing a beer with a cluster Fugget of aromas and flavors. This hop blend was created in celebration of these fortunate mistakes, reminding us that no brew should be taken too seriously. Aroma Profile: Earthy, Grassy, Spicy, Woody

Name: Columbia
Notes: Sibling of Williamette hops. Used for: All English Ales Aroma: Close to Fuggles Substitutes: Fuggles, Williamette

Name: Columbus (Tomahawk)
Notes: Engineered Centennial Substitute - High alpha bittering hops. Used for: Bittering, flavor, aroma - IPA, American Pale Ale, Stout, Lager Aroma: Pungent, spicy. Strongly aromatic, but clean tasting bitterness Substitutes: Centennial

Name: Columbus/Tomahawk/Zeus (CTZ)
Notes: Engineered Centennial Substitute - High alpha bittering hops. Used for: IPA, American Pale Ale, Stout, Lager, Barley Wine Aroma: Pungent, spicy. Strongly aromatic, but clean tasting bitterness Substitutes: Other CTZ, Galena, Chinook, Nugget, Target (U.K.), Northern Brewer (Germany) Storage: Poor/Very Poor (45-65% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 14-16.5% AA / 4-5% Beta

Name: Comet
Notes: Very bitter hops - use sparingly with other hops Used for: Bittering only Substitutes: N/A

Name: Contessa
Notes: A true to type noble hop cultivated in the USA ideal for german styled ales and lagers. Aroma: Herbal - green tea, floral, pear, fruity, citrusy, lemongrass, resinous Alpha Acid: 2.5 - 4.0% Beta Acid: 5.0 - 7.4% Total Oil: 0.8 - 1.9 ml/100g

Name: Cryo - Cascade
Notes: A classic Pacific NW Hops - floral, citrusy, prized in IPAs. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Citra
Notes: Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Eukanot
Notes: An aromatic hop with some tropical, citrus, fruity, and herbal notes. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Loral
Notes: Loral is a floral, citrus, fruity, herbal hops used for its aromatic qualities. Versatile. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Mosaic
Notes: Mosaic is an earthy, floral, fruity hops widely used for finishing IPAs. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Palisade
Notes: A earthy, floral, fruity hop used for aroma including apricot, grass and floral components. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Cryo - Simcoe
Notes: An earthly, citrus, pine hop widely used in IPAs and many American styles. Lupulin powder - typically used during the whirlpool/steep or dry hop phases at about half the weight of pellet hops to mazimize aroma/flavor. Concentrated to enhance hop oils. Store near freezing.

Name: Crystal
Notes: Used for: Aroma and finishing hops for Lagers Aroma: Clean, mild, pleasant and slightly spicy Substitutes: Hallertauer, Mount Liberty

Name: Dana
Notes: Also known as "Extra Styrian Dana" this is a Slovenian hop breed crossing a wild Slovenian male plant with German Magnum. It has smooth bittering properties and a slightly citrus and floral aroma profile.

Name: Defender
Notes: Bred from a New Mexico wild female and Eastwell Golding male at Wye College, England. Low alpha and yield has limited acreage. A pleasant European style aroma.

Name: Delta
Notes: A backcross between Fuggles and Cascade, recently released (2012) by the Hopsteiner breeding program. Woody and herbal with a citrus kick. Not as assertive as dual purpose hops but adds distinctive notes in a blend. Used for: IPA, Pale Ale Aroma: Mild and pleasant aroma; slightly spicy with a hint of citrus. Substitutions: Fuggle, Willamette Storage: Excellent (80-90% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 5-7% AA / 5-7% Beta

Name: Denali (Exp 06277)
Notes: Formerly hopsteiner experimental #06227. Denali is a high alpha aroma hop with heritage to Zeus and Nugget as well as a USDA male. It has a big aroma including pineapple, citrus and pine notes. High essential oil content makes it suitable for dry hopping or whirlpool additions such as IPAs.

Name: Dr. Rudi
Notes: A dual purpose / bittering hop with classic resinous pine and lifted citrus characters. Used for: Ales Substitutions: N/A

Name: East Kent Goldings (EKG)
Notes: Also known as Canterbury Hops by some, although others will dispute this fact. East Kent Goldings gracefully defines the English Pale Ales and Ales produced by the region, it is quintessentially English. Used for: English Ale, Porter, Scottish and Irish Ale, Stout, Strong Ale, ESB, Saison, Barleywine. Aroma: Floral, aromatic, earthy, slightly sweet spicy flavor Substitutes: First Gold, Fuggles, other Golding varieties, Progress, Target, and Whitbread Golding Storage: Good/Fair 965-80% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 4-7% AA / 1.9-2.8% Beta

Name: Eastern Green
Notes: Eastern Green is an aroma variety developed and tested in the 1980’s by the Kirin Brewery Co. Ltd. Hop Research Center in Iwate, Japan. It was conceived via an open pollination cross with named parent varietal, Toyomidori. In addition to its high vigor and yield, it is said to have desirable continental-style aroma traits.

Name: Eclipse
Notes: Eclipse® hops are the newest variety to be released by our friends at Hop Products Australia. Eclipse®, previously known as HPA-016, was created in 2004 and is the daughter of a high alpha Australian and a North American variety. Eclipse® is bright and juicy with mandarin and citrus, making it perfect for IPAs and fruit-forward hazies. Its fresh pine elements also give it a classic West Coast IPA vibe that is killer in trendy Cold IPAs. ANCESTRY Eclipse® was created by the HPA breeding program in 2004 and commercialised in 2020. Its ancestry is the result of cross pollination of high alpha Australian and North American hops. Alpha/Beta Ratio 1.8 - 2.7 Cohumulone 33.0 - 37.0% Total Oils 1.7 - 1.9 ml/100g Oil Concentration 108.0 - 119.0 microlitres of oil/g alpha Myrcene 42% of total oil Linalool 0.6% of total oil Caryophyllene 11.1% of total oil Farnesene 0.2% of total oil Humulene 1.4% of total oil Selinene 17.9% of total oil

Name: Edelweiss
Notes: The Edelweiss hop blend is a distinctive combination of six US-grown hops, specifically crafted to complement a range of beers including pilsners, dorts, lagers, and hefs. This blend is a harmonious fusion of traditional lager hops, with 67% of its flavors reminiscent of German hops and 33% echoing the essence of Czech hops. As a result, when used in brewing, it offers a unique hop flavor that positions the beer somewhere between a German and Bohemian Pilsner. The flavor profile of the Edelweiss hop blend is primarily floral, complemented by a pleasant spiciness that seamlessly melds the flavors together. Flavor/Aroma: Aromatic, Floiral, Fresh, Herbal,Spicy

Name: Ekuanot
Notes: Developed by Hop Breeding Company and released in 2014, Ekuanot® HBC 366 features pronounced aroma characteristics and extremely high oil content. This variety bursts out of the spring soil in vibrant yellow and gradually matures to a deep green color by Fall harvest. Aroma Profile: Bubblegum, Citrus, Stone Fruit, Tropical, Woody

Name: El Dorado
Notes: Bred by CLS Farms, LLC, available on a larger scale for the first time during 2012 harvest. Fits the dual purpose/special mold. Used for: Experimental Ales Aroma: Tropical fruit flavors, intense aromas of stone fruits (pear, cherries), candy (Lifesavers), watermelon. Substitutions: None at this time Storage: Good 14-16% AA / 7-8% Beta

Name: El Dorado Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Extracted from El Dorado hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: El Dorado Hop Hash
Notes: Technically this is lupulin powder similar to Cryo-hops. It is intended for use primarily in the whirlpool, hot steep or for dry hopping. El Dorado brings forward some tropical fruit flavors of pear, watermelon and stone fruit.

Name: Elani
Notes: Elani™, previously known as YQH-1320, is a new hop variety from our friends at Yakima Quality Hops and is available for the first time in 2022. Elani is a found variety that Tim at YQH describes as "Clean and bright. With tropical, citrus, and stone fruit." Sensory analysis of the 2022 crop picks out aromas of pineapple, guava, lime, white peach, and orange zest. Elani Aroma: Pineapple, guava, lime, white peach, orange zest Elani Typical Analytical Range Alpha: 9-11% Total Oil: 1.3 - 2.0 mL/100g

Name: Elixir
Notes: Elixir is a new and exciting hop variety developed in the legendary growing region of Alsace, France. Elixir is a robust and complex hop that offers unique aromas of cognac, leather, and tobacco that are backed by sweet citrus fruits as well rose and lovage. Elixir is well suited for darker styles but can also be incorporated into fruit-forward styles or even spicy saisons. Alpha acid 5-7% Cohumulone 25-30% Beta acids 4.5-5.5% Coluplone 35-40% Total Oil 1.8-2.2 ml/100g Myrcene 70-75% Monoterpene 800-900 mg/100g Sesquiterpene 400-500 mg/100g Linalool 6 mg/100g Farnesene 150-200 mg/100g Geraniol 5-10 mg/100g

Name: Ella
Notes: Ella was formerly known as Stella. Its an Australian variety that is a half sister to Galaxy and related to Spalt. It was released in 2007 for trials and became quite popular. Ella has a very high aromatic oil content that changes depending on how its used. In small quantities it provides a spicy, floral quality like star anise. Larger quantities or dry hopping provide more of a tropical or grapefruit flavor.

Name: Ella (aka Stella)
Notes: New name after a 2013 harvest in Australia (due to name infringement). Despite high alpha, sold as an aroma variety. When used in dry hopping or higher quantities, grapefruit and tropical characters come to the fore. Character obtained at lower rates can be best appreciated in lagers or pilsners, while the grapefruit and tropical flavors can be used to offset robust malt and yeast characters Used for: Ales, Lagers, Pilsners Aroma: tropical, subtle hints of star anise, sweet and floral, with lemon, grapefruit, apricot, and melon. Substitutions: Galaxy Storage: Unknown 13.3-16.3% AA / 4-7.8% Beta

Name: Emerald Spire
Notes: Emerald Spire™ Pleasant, Fruit forward and Delicious. Spire has excellent white grape notes and finishes with a champagne like dryness making it incredibly unique. It's quickly becoming an essential tool for crafting an entire new type of flavor profile in craft beer. Licensed by Great Lakes Hops. Aroma: Peach, Juicy Fruit, White Grape, Watermelon, Bubble Gum, Melon Rind, Champagne

Name: Endeavour
Notes: Bred at Wye Hops in the UK as a cross between Cascade and a hedgerow hop. A wonderful, citrusy hedgerow hop with complex blackcurrant, loganberry and spicy notes as well as grapefruit and lime flavor. Gentler than cascade. Substitutes: Cascade

Name: Epic
Notes: Although brewing experience of this new hop variety is limited, 'Epic' has been reported to give a very pleasant deep, fruity, berry-like aroma with hints of lemongrass. It is slightly herbal without citrus or floral notes. It makes an excellent main copper hop or a late aroma hop. This grower selection was grown as an ornamental garden plant until 2004 when it was propagated to give a small trial area. A consistently good yield has encouraged the planting of a larger commercial area during the winter of 2014-15. Epic was found in 1987 as a chance seedling in a hedge adjacent to a hop field at Bourne Farm, Sandhurst, Kent by Chris Nicholas. The field, although growing 'Wye Target' at the time, had previously grown 'Alliance' and the oil composition of 'Epic' strongly suggests that it is a seedling of 'Alliance'. It is a vigorous tall variety with a heavy yield. Character: Fruity, slightly herbal.

Name: Equinox (HBC 366)
Notes: A pronounced aroma profile with citrus, tropical fruit, floral and herbal characteristics. Specific descriptors include lemon, lime, papaya, apple, and green pepper. Used for: Experimental Ale Aroma: Exceptional aroma profile includes notes of citrus, tropical and pine Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 14.4-15.6% AA / 4.6-5.1% Beta

Name: Ernest
Notes: Ernest is named after its breeder, Prof Ernest Salmon. It was selected at Wye College, Kent as a seedling code OZ97a in 1923 from seed collected in 1921 as an open-pollination of Neomex AA7. It was in production on farm trials in 1957 and 1958. It was used in brewing trials conducted by the Institute of brewing in 1959 (reported in 1960) but it was considered to be a poor substitute for the control variety, Fuggle, due to its "strong, coarse, American aroma". Aroma Profile: Grassy, Herbal, Spicy, Stone Fruit

Name: Eroica
Notes: Used for: General purpose bittering for ales, porters, stouts Aroma: Clean bittering hop, very bitter Substitutes: Galena, Northern Brewer, Chinook Examples: Blackhook Porter, Ballard Bitter

Name: Eukanot
Notes: Released in 2014 - has strong aroma character and a very high aromatic oil content. Aroma profile includes citrus, tropical fruit, fruity and herbal components. Flavors include melon, berry, orange peel, lime, papaya, pine and fresh pepper components.

Name: Eureka!
Notes: Notes: Offspring of Apollo and Merkur. Similar to Simcoe. Dual purpose. Good for dry hoping. Aroma: Herbal, pine, mint, grapefruit rind, tangerine

Name: Evergreen
Notes: Evergreen Hop Blend is a unique hop blend developed to bring out the best citrus, floral, and fruit hop characteristics in American-style craft lagers and ales. After multiple brewing trials at the Rahr Technical Center, the blend was added to the BSG Hop Solutions portfolio in 2021. Evergreen showcases fresh peaches and apricots and bright citrus notes like lemon candy, orange zest, and tangerine. Watermelon candy, fresh hay, and pine needles add a classic American craft beer touch to its aroma. Some secondary and tertiary notes include menthol, dank, geranium, herbs (basil, dill, thyme), cannabis, and green tea. Evergreen is perfect for aroma, whirlpool, and dry-hop additions during or after fermentation. It’s ideal for hoppy lager styles like IPL but also works well in West Coast IPA, pale ale, American black ale, and barley wine. Flavor/Aroma: Peach, Apricot, Lemon, Green Tea, Tangerine

Name: Excelsior
Notes: Excelsior hops are a testament to the rich agricultural diversity of New York, representing the first 100% New York-grown and processed hop blend. This innovative product is the result of a collaboration between the New York Hop Guild and the experts at Hoptechnic, who have meticulously selected and blended the finest hop varieties from the region. The flavor profile of Excelsior hops is complex and tantalizing, marked by tropical mango and lychee notes, complemented by a bouquet of floral aromas and a medley of sweet fruits. This blend is particularly suited for crafting beers where a pronounced hop character is desired, adding a layer of depth and aroma that can elevate pale ales, IPAs, and other hop-forward styles. The unique combination of flavors works harmoniously to provide a sensory experience that is both exotic and familiar, making it a versatile choice for experimental and traditional brewing alike. Flavor/Aroma: Floral, Mango, Lychee, Tropical Fruits, Sweet11/3

Name: Falconer's Flight
Notes: Proprietary pellet blend comprised of many of the Northwest’s most unique hop varieties and is perfect for any Northwest-style IPA. Used for: IPA, Pale Ale, Experimental Ale Aroma: distinct tropical, citrus, floral, lemon and grapefruit tones. Substitutions: Unknown (Citra, Simcoe, Sorachi Ace blend?) Storage: Unknown 10-12% AA / 4.4% Beta

Name: Falconer's Flight 7C's Blend
Notes: A proprietary hop blend from HopUnion that includes 7 of the "C" hops along with a few experimental varieties. Used for: Pale Ale, IPA, Experimental Ales Aroma: Citrus/grapefruit character alongside fruity, spicy, and earthy notes Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 9-10% AA / 4.5% Beta

Name: Fantasia
Notes: A Barth-Haas Group product said to have a flavor/profile of cream and caramel.

Name: Feux Coeur Francais
Notes: Rare, high alpha hops from Australia with genetic roots in the Burgundy region of France. It was adopted to the Victoria region of Australia and introduced in 2010.

Name: Feux-Coeur
Notes: Australian hop with genetic roots back to Burgundy France. Fairly rare breed from Victoria.

Name: First Gold
Notes: Dwarf hop with English Golding character Used for: Ales, ESB Aroma: Similar to other Golding varieties, spicy Substitutes: East Kent Goldings, Crystal

Name: Flyer
Notes: A cross between a high alpha female breeding line and low trellis male. Released for trials in 2009. A citrus hop with aromas of stoned fruit, liquorice, tracle-toffee and caramel with slight burnt notes. Spicy, resinous, citrus and liquorice notes.

Name: Fortnight
Notes: Fortnight™ is a hop blend of five United States hops created by Bintani Australia. Contains a unique blend of grapefruit, pineapple, marmalade, peach, resin and pine. Bintani accomplished this by pairing with Yakima Chief hops that included rigorous trials to get the blend to their liking. Flavor/Aroma: Grapefruit, Pineapple, Marmalade, Peach, Resin, Pine

Name: Fuggle
Notes: Traditional English-type aroma hop. Used for: English Ales, ESB, Bitter, Lagers Aroma: Mild, soft, fruity, spicy, woody Substitute: Fuggle (U.S.), Willamette, Styrian Golding Storage: Good/Very Good (70-80% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 3-5.6% AA 2-3% Beta

Name: Fuggle, New Zealand
Notes: Fuggle is the classic English aroma hop and is often used in conjunction with Goldings. It can be used as a main boiling hop to impart a mild, soft grassy/woody floral aroma and is also used for dry hopping. Used for: English Ales, Porter, Stout, English IPA Substitutions: UK Fuggle, NZ Willamette, NZ Styrian Golding

Name: Fuggle, Organic
Notes: As Fuggle (U.K.), only organic variety Typical alpha: 3-5%. Organically grown Fuggle hops from the UK. Wonderful earthy, pipe-tobacco, floral character. Classic hop for any English ale.

Name: Fuggle, U.S.
Notes: This classic English aroma variety has long been grown in both Oregon and Washington. It has a typical English aroma and contributes a balanced bitterness. Fuggle is very suitable for English and American-style Ales. Mild wood and fruit characteristics. Used for: English Ales Substitutions: Willamette, UK Fuggle, Styrian Golding

Name: Fuggles
Notes: Used for: General purpose bittering/aroma for English Ales, Dark Lagers Aroma: Mild, soft, grassy, floral aroma Substitute: East Kent Goldings, Williamette Examples: Samuel Smith's Pale Ale, Old Peculiar, Thomas Hardy's Ale

Name: Fusion
Notes: Fusion is a aroma hop from Great Britain with moderate alpha acid around 6%. When used in a recipe, it typically accounts for 50% of the hops. British hop which is blend of developmental hops giving fruity/spicy/floral flavours. Flavor/Aroma: Spicy, Tropical Fruits

Name: GP75
Notes: The hop is out of the British Hop collection and was originally from a cross in 1978 between 19/73/3 (a grand-daughter of OR55) and male 321 (a seedling of Neomex). It is wilt tolerant. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Grapefuit, Lime, Pine, Pungent, Sage, Tropical Fruits

Name: Galaxy
Notes: A high alpha, dual purpose seedless hops with a unique hop aroma. It can be used either for bittering or as a late hop addition to provide a flavor of citrus and passionfruit to the finished beer. Aroma: Intense, pleasant, unique.

Name: Galena
Notes: Used for: General bittering hops for all beers Aroma: Strong, clean, balanced bittering Substitutes: Eroica, Northern Brewer, Cluster, Chinook Examples: Catamount Porter

Name: Gargoyle
Notes: Gargoyle along with Ivanhoe are the first generation of California Cluster hops to be grown commercially in over 50 years. Revived by Hops-Mesiter near Clearlake CA. Similar to Ivanhoe but has a more citrus-mango bouquet. Substitute: Ivanhoe

Name: Gemini
Notes: Gemini™ is quickly becoming known as this years ultimate utility power house. This Hop is no "one trick pony", offering endless opportunities to enhance and expand the flavor profile of a brew. Licensed by Great Lakes Hops. Aroma: Strawberry, Eucalyptus, Rosemary, Fruit Cocktail​, Honey Dew

Name: German Tradition
Notes: Close descendant of Hallertauer Mittelfrueh bred for high yield. Used for: Lager, Pilsner, Bock, some Weizens Aroma: Medium intense floral and herbal (grassy) tones Substitutes: Hallertau Mittelfruh, Liberty, Hersbrucker Storage: Unknown 5-7% AA / 4-5% Beta

Name: Gesho Kitel
Notes: Brundo's aroma hops Gesho stems, gathered from the highlands of Ethiopia, are sun-dried and packed fresh. These aromatic sticks and stem flavor centuries old traditional brewing recipes for beer and Ethiopian honey wine. Acid content unknown.

Name: Glacier
Notes: Genetic mix of at least 8 hops including Elsasser, Brewers Gold, N Brewer, Bullion, Early Green, and others...released in 2000. Used for: Aroma Aroma: Excellent, hoppy Substitutes: N/A

Name: Godiva
Notes: Godiva™ is from the Charles Faram Hop Development Programme. Good for bittering and excels when used in late hopping. A daughter of Jester®, selected for its aroma, disease resistance and yield Character: Spicy, Gooseberry, Tangerine

Name: Gold
Notes: Daughter of Styrian Savinsjki Golding released in 2009. Distinct aroma grown only in Slovenia. Aroma of honey, oregano, basil, nettle and hay.

Name: Golden Star
Notes: Golden Star has heritage of Saaz and White Vine-OP. Selected by Dr. Y. Mori of Sapporo Brewery, Japan from a mutant of Shinshuwase, making it nearly identical. Commercially only grown in Japan as an aroma hop that is difficult to pick as the cones shatters easily.

Name: Golding, New Zealand
Notes: Mild, delicate sweet floral character Used for: English Ales Substitutions: NZ Willamette, NZ Styrian Golding, NZ Fuggle, UK East Kent Golding

Name: Golding, U.S.
Notes: Mild and delicate with sweet floral characteristics. Bred in the US from east Kent Golding. Golding hops consist of a group of traditional English aroma varieties which have been cultivated since 1790. Several selections now exist including Cobbs, Early Bird, East Well, Bramling, Canterbury and Mathon, some of which are now being grown in the U. S. All Goldings are recognized as having a most typical English aroma. Used for: Bitter Substitutions: Fuggle, Willamette, UK East Kent Golding, Styrian Golding

Name: Goldings, B.C.
Notes: Used for: Bittering and finishing British ales, bitters, porters and stouts. Aroma: Spicy, floral, rounded mild aroma. Substitutes: East Kent Goldings, Fuggles

Name: Goldings, East Kent
Notes: Used for: General purpose hops for bittering/finishing all British Ales Aroma: Floral, aromatic, earthy, slightly sweet spicy flavor Substitutes: Fuggles, BC Goldings Examples: Bass Pale Ale, Fullers ESB, Samual Smith's Pale Ale

Name: Green Bullet
Notes: Bittering hops from New Zealand developed in 1972 Used for: Australian-style ales and lagers. Aroma: Floral, raisin aroma. Substitutes: Styrian Goldings

Name: Greensburg
Notes: American hop variety from southern Idaho. Fruity flavor with a touch of wood flavor. Popular with microbreweries.

Name: Grüngeist (Callista)
Notes: This German aroma variety was developed in the Hop Research Center Hüll (Hopfenforschungszentrum Hüll). This hop research institution focuses on breeding, harvesting, and chemistry. It is located in the largest continuous hop-planting region in the world, Hallertau Germany in the state of Bavaria. The Hop Research Society was founded in 1926 in response to an endemic of downy mildew in Germany. The Hüll Institute is also responsible for recently giving us great varieties like Hallertau Blanc, Mandarina Bavaria, Hull Melon. Grungeist is an aroma hop that is typically used in only late boil additions, including dry hopping. The unique combination of peach and passion fruit are equally balanced with a hint of lemon zest and floral essence. Grüngeist adds aromas and flavors of kiwi, overripe peach and dried lavender

Name: HBC 291 (Experimental)
Notes: As this is an experimental variety, there is no guarantee this hop will come to market en masse. Data obtained from HopUnion Uses: Unknown Aroma: Typically described as peppery, floral and fruity Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 11.2-13.1% AA / 5.1-6.1% Beta

Name: HBC 342 (Experimental)
Notes: As this is an experimental variety, there is no guarantee this hop will come to market en masse. Data obtained from HopUnion. "High alpha variety with a pleasant aroma" Uses: Pale Ale Aroma: Mild citrus, tropical fruit and melon notes Substitutions: Unknown (Belma or Citra?) Storage: Unknown ??% AA / ??% Beta

Name: HBC 366 (Experimental)
Notes: As this is an experimental variety, there is no guarantee this hop will come to market en masse. Data obtained from HopUnion. "The fantastic aroma combined with the high high oils make this hop very unique" Uses: Experimental Ale Aroma: Exceptional aroma profile includes notes of citrus, tropical and pine Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 14.4-15.6% AA / 4.6-5.1% Beta

Name: HBC 438
Notes: An experimental hop that is a cross between a neomexicanus and YCR 123. It is sister to HBC 472 and has similar character. Aromas include mango, coconut, grapefruit, lemon grass and wood. Peachy stone fruit, pineapple., lime and herbal notes of cedar and coconut. A disctinct hop. Subsitute: HBC 472

Name: Hallertau
Notes: This traditional German land-race variety has limited acreage in the U.S. Used for: lager, pilsner, bock, wheat, Munich Helles, or Belgian style. Aroma: considered mild, pleasant, and slightly flowery & spicy. Substitutions: Liberty, German Hallertau, German Tradition Storage: Fair (50-60% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 3.5-5.5% AA / 3.5-5.5% Beta

Name: Hallertau Blanc
Notes: Floral and fruity with passion fruit, grapefruit, pineapple, grape and lemongrass overtones. One of several new German varieties with bold flavor profiles, perfect for American-style Ales. Used for: American style ale Substitutions: N/A Storage Unknown

Name: Hallertau Brewers Gold
Notes: A high-alpha hop providing a pronounced bitterness with notes of lemon, currant, and spice. Aroma: Citrus - currant, lemon, spicy, woody, herbal, floral, resinous Alpha Acid: 5.0 - 7.0% Beta Acid: 2.5 - 4.5% Total Oil: 1.5 - 2.2 ml/100g

Name: Hallertau Magnum
Notes: German Hallertauer hybrid, widely used in Germany. Extremely appropriate for early boil additions. Used for: German ales and lagers, bitters, IPAs, pilsners, pale ales, stouts. Aroma: somewhat unnoticeable aroma; stable spicy flavor Substitutes: Hallertau Taurus, Columbus, Nugget Storage: Excellent (80%-85% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 12-14% AA / 4.5-5.5% Beta

Name: Hallertau Perle
Notes: A dual-purpose hop that offers great bittering properties as well as a well-rounded aroma profile. Aroma: Herbal - tea, pepper, floral, citrus, earthy, woody, grassy, caramel Alpha Acid: 4.0 - 9.0% Beta Acid: 2.5 - 4.5% Total Oil: 0.5 - 1.3 ml/100g

Name: Hallertau Taurus
Notes: Bred at the Huell Hops Institute in Germany and prided in its bittering qualities. The bittering is moderately abundant and this variety adds a pleasant aroma as well. Used for: Schwawrz, Oktoberfest, Lager Aroma: seasoned, flavorful, zesty character Substitutions: Hallertau Magnum, German Tradition Storage: Fair/Good (60%-70% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 12.3-17.9% AA / 4-6% Beta

Name: Hallertauer
Notes: Used for: German Ales, German/US/Canadian Lagers, Wheat Beers Aroma: Pleasant, mild spicy flavor, clean, neutral flavor Substitutes: Crystal, Liberty, Hallertauer Mittelfrueh

Name: Hallertauer Hersbrucker
Notes: Used for: German style lagers and wheat beers Aroma: Noble, pleasant, clean flavor Substitutes: Mt. Hood, Liberty, Hallertauer, Mittelfrueh Examples: Wheathook Wheaten Ale

Name: Hallertauer Mittelfrueh
Notes: Used for: Continental Lagers, German Ales/Lagers, US Lagers Aroma: Pleasant, assertive, mild herbal aroma Substitutes: Hallertauer, Mt. Hood, Liberty, Crystal Examples: Sam Adams Boston Lager, Sam Adams Boston Lightship

Name: Hallertauer Taurus
Notes: German born Hallertau Taurus features an earthy, strong aroma with hints of chocolate and banana, spice and pepper and finishes with a zesty curry undertone. It was released in 1995. Interestingly, it carries the highest xanthohumol content of any hop. Xanthohumol is a potent antioxidant, 200 times more powerful than antioxidants found in red wine. Used in Paulaner’s hop selection for their Oktoberfest bier.

Name: Hallertauer, New Zealand
Notes: Hallertauer variant with classic aroma and ability to retain clean taste. Bred from German Hallertauer Mittelfrueh Used for: Australian Ales Substitutes: Hallertauer Mittelfrueh

Name: Harlequin
Notes: Harlequin® is a newer hop variety to be released by the Britain-based Charles Faram Hop Development Programme. Harlequin® is an intense hop that boasts big fruit flavors such as passion fruit, peach, and pineapple. Harlequin® is the daughter of another one of Charles Faram's varieties called Godiva® and not only has an incredible aroma and big brewing potential, but was also selected for its disease resistance and good yield. The breeder considers Harlequin® to be a dual-purpose hop, but it seems to excel especially in late hop additions, whirlpooling, and dry hopping. Because Harlequin is such a new variety we are looking to see what you think of it. So be sure to let us know what you brew up with it and how it turns out! Harlequin Hop Profile Aroma: Passion fruit, Peach, Pineapple Pedigree: Daughter of Godiva® that was selected for its aroma, disease resistance, and yield. So it has a lot going for it. Cohumulone: 27 - 31 % of alpha acids Total Oil: 1.0 - 1.8 ml/100g Myrcene: 55 - 65 % of total oil Humulene: 0 - 10 % of total oil Farnesene: 5 - 15 % of total oil Flavor Intensity: 9/10

Name: Harmonie
Notes: Harmonie - is characteristic by a high share of beta fraction but oppose to Sládek it has higher content of alpha bitter acids. The variety was registered in 2004 and therefore the acreage is currently very limited. The first brewing trials and testing in Czech breweries showed at very good quality especially for intensity and quality of beer aroma.

Name: Hartwick
Notes: Hartwick hops, trademarked by Great Lakes Hops, are an aroma variety cultivated in the United States of America. Originating as a "field grade" hop by Great Lake Hops, Hartwick is still in its developmental stages and is not yet ready for widespread commercialization. This is primarily due to pest-related issues that the plant faces. When it comes to its flavor and aroma profile, Hartwick hops are distinctively piney with notes of overripe pineapple. Great Lakes Hops (GLH) emphasizes that the aroma of Hartwick is notably different from their other field grade hops. Typically, Hartwick hops are used in the late boil additions of the brewing process, including dry hopping, to impart its unique aroma to the beer. Flavor/Aroma: Pine, Pineapple

Name: Helga
Notes: Helga is an Australian cultivar, formerly known as Southern Hallertau. Helga was bred by Hop Products Australia from Hallertau Mittelfrüher in an effort to dissect and retain its Hallertau’s characteristic noble aroma. Helga is easy to work with—no matter at what point she is added into the brew, even when dry hopping, the results are said to be ever pleasant. It is mildly floral and spicy. Helga made a quiet debut, established in 1996 it took nearly 10 years to catch on commercially. Currently, production is being increased as craft brewers take interest.

Name: Helga (Southern Hallertau)
Notes: Perviously known as Southern Hallertau. Bears almost no aroma similarities to it's European cousin. Very unique aroma with moderate bittering. Used for: Austrialian and English-style ales. Aroma: Ddelicate floral and subtle herbal aroma Substitutes: Unknown (possible Nobel Hop variety) Storage: Unknown 5-6% AA / 3.8-5.4% Beta

Name: Helios
Notes: Super high alpha variety with clean, pleasant bitterness. Aroma: Resinous - Stone fruit, pine, spicy Alpha Acid: 15.5 - 18.5% Beta Acid: 3.5 - 4.5% Total Oil: 1.0 - 2.0 ml/100g

Name: Herald
Notes: Dwarf variety bred at Wye College (mid 1990s) Used for: Bittering of ales Aroma: Strong, acceptable Substitutes: High alpha English bittering hops

Name: Herkules
Notes: A German high alpha cross variety, bred in Huell and released in 2005. Imparts robust, hoppy tang flavors. Used for: hoppy German-style ales or lagers Aroma: Spicy floral and hop notes, with hints of pine, lemon and black pepper. Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Good 13-17% AA / 4-5.5% Beta

Name: Hersbrucker
Notes: Hersbrucker Hops is similar to the noble hop varieties with its low co-humulone level. Aroma: Classicly spicy, herbal, with hints of citrus and stone fruit. Used for: German and Bavarian style Lagers, Pilsners, Hefeweizens, Dunkles, and Bocks Substitutions: Mt. Hood, Strisselspalt, Hallertau Mittelfruh, Liberty Storage: Fair (55%-65% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 2-5% AA / 4-6% Beta

Name: Hersbrucker E, England
Notes: Hersbrucker E (for England) was planted and grown at Wye College, England in the 1970’s. It is largely identical to the original Hersbrucker variety grown in the Hersbruck Mountains. The storage stability, especially of its soft resins, is low.

Name: Hokitika
Notes: Hokitika® is of European origin but was first grown in New Zealand in 1882. The hop originated from a hop garden that was started in the 1880's by the brewery principal, WCJ Kortegast, at Kortegast's Brewery at Gibson Quay, on the Hokitika River. Those gardens had been growing wildly for 136 years. Flavor/Aroma: Floral, Honey, Nectar, Spicy

Name: HopShot
Notes: CO2-extracted hop resin that can be used for bittering or late additions to boiling wort. Primarily for use in boil. Made from Columbus (or CTZ) blend hops. http://www.northernbrewer.com/documentation/hopshot.pdf

Name: HopShot
Notes: CO2-extracted hop resin that can be used for bittering or late additions to boiling wort. Primarily for use in boil. Made from Columbus (or CTZ) blend hops. http://www.northernbrewer.com/documentation/hopshot.pdf

Name: Horizon
Notes: Good dual purpose hops derived from Nugget hops in Oregon (1970) Used for: All purpose Ales, Lagers Aroma: Pleasant, hoppy Substitutes: Magnum or other high alpha hops

Name: Huell Melon
Notes: Distinct fruit characteristics including honeydew melon and strawberry flavors. An excellent consideration for varieties not necessarily associated with typical hop flavors. Substitutions: N/A Storage Unkonwn

Name: Hulla Norrgård
Notes: Hulla Norrgård is a very old Swedish hop, the earliest map showing where it's grown is from 1634. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Clove, Herbal, Spicy

Name: Hydra
Notes: Hydra™ Originally nicknamed "Befuddlement" for it's unique yet unidentifiable characteristics has emerged with an incredible ability to amplify and juice up a brew. Hydra can not only accentuate other hops characteristics but can easily shine solo. Aroma: Bright Citrus, Tangerine, Mandarin, Clementine, Green Grape, Orange Rind​ Licensed by Great Lakes Hops

Name: Ibuki
Notes: Ibuki hops are an intriguing variety that originated from the Iwate and Miyagi regions in Japan. As an improved version of the Shinshuwase hops, Ibuki was developed through a crossbreeding process that combined the noble characteristics of the German Saaz hops with the robust qualities of the American White Vine. The flavor profile of Ibuki hops is primarily floral, with a pure and pronounced aroma that can influence the beer's bouquet even when used sparingly. This makes Ibuki hops particularly suitable for styles where a delicate yet noticeable hop character is desired. Whether used in traditional Japanese styles or integrated into more familiar Western brews, these hops bring a touch of Japanese terroir to the brewing process, allowing brewers to experiment with geographical nuances in their beer crafting. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Earthy, Floral, Fresh, Herbal, Noble

Name: Idaho #7
Notes: Notes: Dual purpose, high alpha acid Aroma: Tropical fruit, orange, apricot, pine.

Name: Idaho #7 Hop Hash
Notes: Technically this is lupulin powder similar to Cryo-hops. It is intended for use primarily in the whirlpool, hot steep or for dry hopping. Idaho #7 has a black tea charcter and fresh herbal aroma.

Name: Independence
Notes: Independence Hops are a proprietary blend created by Yakima Chief Hops. This blend originates from the merging of old-world and new-world hop varieties, reflecting the pioneering spirit of generations of growers in the Pacific Northwest. Harvested in mid-to-late August, Independence is primarily used as an aroma hop in late boil additions and dry hopping. The flavor and aroma profile of the Independence hop blend combines bold, earthy notes such as tobacco, pine, and grass with more delicate touches of citrus, herbs, and stone fruit. Its versatile profile offers floral and herbal undertones, making it perfect for adding depth and character to various beer styles. Independence hop blend can be used in a variety of beer styles, such as American Pale Ales, IPAs, and Amber Ales. This hop’s robust flavor also pairs well with more experimental brews like Saisons and New England IPAs. Additionally, its versatile nature makes it suitable for enhancing malt-forward styles, such as Brown Ales and Stouts. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Floral, Earthy, Herbal, Pine, Stone Fruit, Tobacco

Name: Iunga
Notes: Unleash the potential of your brews with Iunga, a versatile dual-purpose hop that effortlessly marries bold bitterness with an intoxicating aroma. Bred through the artful union of Lubelski and a Yugoslavian male, Iunga stands as a testament to the harmonious fusion of heritage and innovation. Flavor Profile: Iunga captivates the palate with a swift burst of short-lived bitterness, courtesy of its robust alpha-acid content. What truly sets it apart is the exquisite aroma it imparts – a tantalizing symphony of pineapple, peach, and citrusy notes that dance on the taste buds. Aroma: Immerse your brew in the tropical allure of Iunga, where strong hints of pineapple transport you to sun-drenched islands, complemented by the succulence of peach and the zesty brightness of citrus. This hop is a sensory journey, promising a multi-dimensional flavor experience.

Name: Ivanhoe
Notes: A revival of the old California Cluster variety. It has a European aroma that is more moderate than Cascade and has citrus, piney and heady floral and herbal notes. Substitute: Cascade or Cluster

Name: Izabella
Notes: Meet Izabella, a hidden gem from the hop archives, developed in 1988 alongside Marynka during an era when high-alpha varieties were the sought-after stars. Unfortunately, Izabella’s journey was short-lived, as its bittering potential didn’t meet the demands of the big players. Farming ceased, and it was even removed from the Polish variety catalogue. Rediscovering Izabella revealed its citrusy and fresh aroma, a testament to the resilience of forgotten treasures. Flavor: Izabella, despite its brief stint in the limelight, leaves an indelible mark with its distinct flavor profile. The citrusy and fresh aromas, hidden for years, now emerge as a tribute to a bygone era. Izabella showcases the potential of overlooked varieties, reminding us that every hop has its own story to tell. Aroma: Embark on a sensory journey with Izabella, where the essence of lime and citrus takes center stage. The aroma is a testament to the resilience of this nearly forgotten variety, offering a refreshing and invigorating experience. Izabella’s reemergence highlights the untapped potential that lies within the rich tapestry of hop history.

Name: Jarrylo
Notes: Notes: Underwhelming hop. Good if used in combination of hops with stronger flavors like Amarillo. Aroma: Banana, pear, spice.

Name: Jester
Notes: Developed by Charles Faram & Co, a proprietary hop different than most UK hops. Aromatic notes of tropical fruit, grapefruit, blackcurrent and Lychee. A late addition or dry hop, but not recommended for bittering in the boil. Closer to a New World hop style.

Name: Junga
Notes: A high alpha bittering hop from Poland similar in some ways to German Magnum but with a nuce aroma. Derived from a Saaz variety. Substitute: Nugget, German Magnum or Target

Name: Kazbek
Notes: From the Czech Republic, released in 2008. The classic noble spice of saaz, but with a earthy lemon peel tone. Primarily an aroma hop for whirlpool or dry hopping. Noble hop character but high oil content. Similar to a very strong Saaz with notes of citrus and grapefruit. Substitute: Saaz

Name: Kirin II
Notes: A Japenese hop that grew from Japan's decision to reduce hop imports. Grown domestically in Japan under government contract. Developed by Kirin Brewery, it is a direct descendant of Saaz and White Vine.

Name: Klon 18
Notes: Klon-18 hops, originating from Ukraine, have a rich history deeply rooted in the brewing traditions of Eastern Europe. With its introduction in 1936, Klon-18 quickly became a staple in the brewing industry, prized for its versatile nature and consistent quality. The hop's cultivation in regions historically part of Poland contributes to its resemblance to Lublin hops. Homebrewers favor Klon-18 for its dual-purpose characteristics, making it suitable for both bittering and aroma purposes. When utilized in brewing, Klon-18 imparts a delightful array of flavors, including a pleasant fruity essence accompanied by grassy and floral undertones. These flavor profiles make it particularly well-suited for brewing lagers, where its subtle yet distinctive notes complement the crispness of the style. Whether used as a primary hop or in combination with others, Klon-18 adds complexity and depth to a variety of beer recipes, enhancing the overall drinking experience. Flavor/Aroma: Crisp, Floral, Fruity, Grassy, Pleasant

Name: Kohatu
Notes: Moderate aroma with lime and pineapple with traces of pine. Overuse can exhibit "dank" character. Used for: Belgian Ales, Wheat Beer, Blonde Ale Substitutions: N/A

Name: Korsta
Notes: The Korsta hop traces its origins to the village of "Korsta", located near Sundsvall. In this village, the hop was discovered growing adjacent to a site that was marked as a hop farm on a parcel map dating back to 1769. This discovery not only highlights the hop's longevity in the region but also its enduring connection to the brewing traditions of the area. It suggests a lineage that might even predate the Vikings. Historically, hops were not just limited to beer brewing, but was also a favored ingredient in mead and braggot, two beverages that have been enjoyed for centuries. The aroma of the Korsta hop is a delightful blend of spicy and citrusy notes. The grapefruit scent is particularly dominant, complemented by hints of tropical fruits and the fresh scent of green grass. For the homebrewer looking to experiment with a hop that carries with it a tale as old as time, Korsta is an excellent choice. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Earthy, Grapefruit, Grassy, Herbal, Spicy, Tropical Fruit, Zesty

Name: Kracanup
Notes: The Kracanup hop is an exciting new addition to the world of brewing, originating from Western Australia. This hop is the brainchild of a semi-retired couple, Colin and Britt Butler, who ventured into hop farming with little prior knowledge about hops. Kracanup is a hybrid hop variety, resulting from a cross between the renowned Cascade and Chinook hops, both of which have their roots in the US and have significantly influenced the West Coast American Pale Ale style. This hop has garnered attention from several West Coast beer enthusiasts. Flavor/Aroma: Fruity

Name: Książęcy
Notes: A sensational ressurected hop from PolishHops that is making waves in the brewing world. A newcomer that brings a burst of freshness to the scene, Książęcy boasts an intense coconut aroma, complemented by notes of red orange and apricot. Książęcy, a herald of the new wave hops, brings a refreshing and fruity aroma that can elevate your beer at every hopping stage. With an alpha acid content of 6.20%, this hop introduces a delightful interplay of coconut, red orange, apricot, pineapple, mango, and tangerine, creating a sensory experience that transcends traditional boundaries. Aroma: Immerse yourself in the captivating aroma of Książęcy, where the essence of coconut takes center stage, accompanied by the vibrant notes of red orange, apricot, pineapple, mango, and tangerine. This hop is a symphony of tropical and citrusy delights, adding a layer of complexity to your brew.

Name: Lambic
Notes: A triploid hybrid of the English Fuggle released in 1976 from the USDA breeding program. Lambic is an aroma hop that is typically used in only late boil additions, including dry hopping

Name: Legacy
Notes: Legacy has been cultivated in Washington state for over 50 years, but was registered and named in 2013. It has some wild notes of blackberry, black currant, orange, grapefruit and florap and spicy notes.

Name: Lemondrop
Notes: Notes: Good for citrusy IPA, APA, IPL, or Saisons. Should be used with a hop with more distinct flavor that would benefit from the lemon flavor. Aroma: Lemony, floral, earthy, herbal, grassy, pine, pepper

Name: Liberty
Notes: Used for: German style lagers Aroma: Fine, mild, slightly spicy flavor Substitutes: Hallertau, Mt Hood, Crystal Examples: Pete's Wicked Lager

Name: Liberty, New Zealand
Notes: Mild and spicy, subtle lemon and citrus. Used in many styles. Contributes good bitterness in the beginning of the boil and an aroma of grapes, peaches, and vanilla at the end of the boil. Derived from Hallertau Mittlefruh.

Name: Limonadier
Notes: Limonadier hops are a testament to the innovative spirit of the craft brewing movement, particularly emerging from the fertile grounds of Quebec. Originating from the Double LL farm in St-Édouard-de-Lotbinière, these hops are a proud product of Canadian agriculture, boasting a lineage that includes the popular Cascade variety. As a dual-purpose hop, Limonadier is versatile in the brewing process, suitable for both bittering and aroma additions in a variety of beer styles such as Pale Ale, IPA, Saison, and Trappist. The flavor profile of Limonadier is distinctively citrus-forward, with a pronounced lemon character that inspired its name. This lemon essence is complemented by notes of grapefruit, adding a zesty complexity to the hop's profile. Beyond the citrus realm, Limonadier surprises with a peculiar blend of very ripe mango and resinous pine, creating a "fruit bomb" sensation that can elevate the sensory experience of any brew. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Fruity, Grapefruit, Lemon, Mango, Pine, Zesty

Name: Loral
Notes: Released in 2016, Loral HBC 291 has a noble heritage and crosses old world and new world aromatics. Pleasant aroma including floral, pepper, lemon citrus and dark fruit notes. Suitable for many beer styles.

Name: Lubelska
Notes: Lubelska has heritage from Saaz which come through in the fine aroma characteristics. Despite being mostly used by Polish breweries, this hop originated in Zatec, Czechoslovakia. It is now widely used in Poland.

Name: Lubelska-Pulawy
Notes: First entering commercial production in 1964, Lubelska-Pulawy is a Polish-grown variety descended from the original Czech hop Lubelska, or Lublin as it is sometimes known. It was grown by the Hop Institute in Pulawy, Poland in an effort to better mold the variety to local conditions and increase alpha potential. It is said to have pleasant European characteristics, unsurprising given its Saaz roots.

Name: Lubelski
Notes: Lubelski hops are among the super-aromatic varieties: alpha-acids about 5.0% . This extremely aromatic hop is used in brewing bottom and top fermentation beers as well as for hopping during silent fermentation. Very well known among Belgian brewers. It is widely believed to belong to the Saaz family. However, there is a legend circulating that says it is a wild hop adapted to mass cultivation. Thanks to its unusual aroma, it is the second most popular hop in Poland and has been cultivated since 1964. It has herbal aromas complemented by notes of cinnamon, bergamot and lemon. Mainly used in bottom fermentation beers.

Name: Lublin
Notes: Version of Saaz grown in Poland - also called "Lubelski" Used for: Bohemian lagers and Pilsners Aroma: Noble, mild flavor similar to Saaz Substitutes: Saaz, Tettnanger

Name: Lublin (Lubelski)
