Notes: Lublin, also called "Lubelski", was bred from Saaz and carries with it many of the same characteristics. Used for: Bohemian lagers and Pilsners Aroma: Noble, mild flavor similar to Saaz Substitutes: Saaz, Tettnanger, Sterling Storage: Good (70%-75% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 3-5% AA / 2.5-4% Beta

Name: Luminosa
Notes: Luminosa hops are a testament to the innovative spirit of the craft brewing world, particularly the Indie Hops Flavor Project, which has brought this vibrant variety to the forefront with its 2022 Harvest debut. The brainchild of Shaun Townsend from Oregon State University's hop breeding program, Luminosa hops were born from open-pollinated seeds of a Sorachi Ace female in 2013. It delivers a burst of tropical and citrus notes, reminiscent of a refreshing peach-mango lemonade, enriched with layers of candied orange peel, boysenberry, papaya, and guava. Luminosa hops offer an exciting opportunity to experiment with flavor without the risk of overpowering their brew with the piney and resinous notes that can dominate the palate. This makes it an excellent choice for a wide range of beer styles, from the crispness of a Helles or Pilsner to the depth of an American Pale Ale, the haze of an IPA, or the lightness of a Blonde or Wheat Ale. Aroma: Citrus, Fruity, Guava, Mango, Orange, Papaya, Peach, Tropical

Name: Lórien
Notes: Lórien hops, a new addition to the hop family, have been developed with a specific vision in mind: to complement and enhance the crisp, refreshing qualities of lager beers. As the third commercial hop launch from Indie Hops, Lórien is a progeny of a Sterling female and a German male hop, embodying a blend of Old World and New World hop characteristics. The flavor profile of Lórien hops is distinct and multifaceted, offering a bouquet of lemon and lime zest, fresh melon, sweet hay, and wildflowers, with a unique finish of cinnamon spice that acts as a palate cleanser. The hop's ability to let the malt shine through while still providing a complex finish makes it a versatile ingredient for various beer styles, though it shines brightest in lagers, light beer styles, and ales. It pairs well with classic lager hops like Hallertauer, Hersbrucker, and Czech Saaz, it can be used to enrich the flavor profile without overpowering the essential "beer" flavor that comes from the malt and clean fermentation. Flavor/Aroma: Cinnamon, Floral, Hay, Lemon, Lime, Melon

Name: Mackinac
Notes: Mackinac™ executes wide utility and flavorful new beer styles that are trending toward these aromas. The moderately low Cohumulone offers brewers a smoother brewing profile with excellent foam characteristics. It has good resistance to disease and insect pressure and is above average in vigor and yield for this class of hops. This hop was rated in the top 10% by both the brewer's trials and grower trials for new varieties in 2013. Licensed by Great Lakes Hops. Aroma: BIG Tropical, Citrus, Melon, Papaya, Spice​

Name: Magnum
Notes: High-alpha hop with clean, neutral bitterness. Can be used in almost any recipe. Used for: Ale, Lager, Stout, hefeweizen, IPA, Bitter Aroma: No distinctive aroma characteristics Substitutions: German Magnum, CTZ, Nugget Storage: Excellent/Very good (80%-85% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 10-14% AA / 4.5-7% Beta

Name: Malling
Notes: The British Malling Mid-season hop variety is not grown commercially in UK where it was bred. however, it is one of Austria’s more popular hop varieties where it is simply known as Malling . It is a bittering hop but also popular for it’s mainly fruity aromas with soft mint and peppery notes added in. Flavor/Aroma: Apricot, Citrus, Melon, Pear, Pepper

Name: Mandarina Bavaria
Notes: One of four new varieties released to German growers in 2012. Shares characteristics of its mother, Cascade, but fruitier and more herbal. Used for: American pale ale, lager (as late hop) Aroma: Distinct tangerine and citrus flavors Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 7-10% AA / 4-7% Beta

Name: Mapuche
Notes: Grown in the Upper Black River Valley (39th parallel) in Argentina, Flavor/Aroma: Grapefruit, Blueberry, Red Fruit, Peach, Apricot

Name: Marynka
Notes: A widely produced Polish hop from the Lublin area. Offspring of Brewer's Gold registered in 1988. Excellent flavor profile. Used primarily for bittering though it also features earthy, herbal flavors and aromas.

Name: Mathon White
Notes: A very old varitety which may have been bred from Farnham Whitebine or Caturbury Whitebine like Goldings was. A pleasant aroma similar to Goldings. Goes well in most any English style. Substitute: Kent Goldings or Bramling

Name: McKenzie
Notes: McKenzie™ hops are the first variety to be released by the West Coast Hop Breeding Company out of Oregon. McKenzie™ was specifically bred to be grown in Oregon and highlight the area's terroir. McKenzie™ is "bright and fruity" with grapefruit, lemon, nectarine, and melon aromas that is backed by pine, resin, and wood. Fits really well in the classic West Coast IPA or American Pale Ale, but also plays well in fruit-forward and Hazy IPAs. McKenzie™ Aroma: Grapefruit, lemon, nectarine, melon, pine resin, thyme. McKenzie™ Typical Analytical Range Alpha: 9-11% Beta: 8-9% Total Oil: 2.0-3.0 mL/100g 2021 is the first crop year for McKenzie™ hops.

Name: Medusa
Notes: A native hop of Colorado and New Mexico that is literally an entirely different species - humulus lupulus neomexicanus from most hops. Strong flavor and aroma character of guava, melon, apricot, and citrus fruit. Excellent in IPAs as whirlpool or dry hop addition.

Name: Melba
Notes: Called the "Dame of Hops", Melba is a dual purpose hop from Australia's Ellerslie Hop Estate. It has a high oil content and moderate alpha acid making it suitable for late and dry hop additions. Adds a spicy bitterness. Aromatics of passion fruit, grapefruit, and citrus. Comparable to Galaxy.

Name: Meridian
Notes: Grown primarily in Willamette Valley - a unique variety with crisp flavors of lemon, mixed berry and tropical fruit. Complements many other hop flavors very well. Some notes of nectarine, apricots, with some pine. Use in: Belgians, IPAs, Blondes. Substitute: Citra, Glacier, Centennial

Name: Merkur (Hallertauer Merkur)
Notes: Sometimes called Hallertauer Merkur, this variety was bred from a cross between Hallertaur Magnum and variety 81/8/13 from the Hull Hops Research Institute. Mostly used for bittering qualities, appropriate for early to mid additions. Aroma: Strong earthy, floral and citrus impressions Substitutions: Maganum (U.S.), Hallertau Magnum Storage: Fair/Good (60%-70% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 12-16.2% AA / 5.1% Beta

Name: Michigan Copper
Notes: Michigan Copper™ was among the first new cultivar to be released from the GLH trials and breeding program in 2014. It has gained traction and praise across the United States since and has only increased in demand. Aroma: Fruit Punch, Tropical, Pepper, Cherry, Floral Licensed by the Great Lakes Hops program.

Name: Millenium
Notes: Originally developed as part of the Haas breeding program in the US, Millenium entered production in 2000. It is a very high alpha hops used for bittering. Aroma: Mild, herbal Substitutes: Columbus, Tomahawk, Zeus and Nugget

Name: Minstrel
Notes: Minstrel® is a low trellis wilt resistant variety from the Charles Faram Hop Development Programme and is tolerant to powdery mildew. High Farnasene gives beers brewed with Minstrel® great fullness of flavour and depth. Character: Spicy, berries, orange citrus.

Name: Mistral
Notes: Mistral is a new and exciting hop variety developed in the legendary growing region of Alsace, France. Mistral offers a refreshing character of citrus fruits, floral, and sweet fruits. Alpha acid 6.5-8.5 % Cohumulone29-39 % Beta acids3.1-3.8 % Coluplone51-54 % Total Oil1-1.5 ml/100g Myrcene59-65 % Humulene9.5-12.8 % Monoterpene72-79 % Sesquiterpene20.8-27.5 % Humulene / Caryophyllene3-3. 15 % Linalool5-6 mg/100g Farnesene2.5-3.5 mg/100g

Name: Monroe
Notes: A new hop variety from Barth-Haas group. Only 2.5% apha but a fairly high oil content. Mild and smooth, full of red aromas including raspberry notes and orange syrup.

Name: Mosaic (HBC 369)
Notes: Available in quantity for the first time after 2012 harvest. Still known to many as HBC 369. A daughter of Simcoe crossed with a Nugget derived male. An excellent aroma variety. Used for: Ales, IPA, Experimental Ales Aroma: Rich in mango, lemon, citrus, pine, and notably, blueberry. Substitutes: Unknown Storage: Good (25% AA after 6 months at 20 C) 11-13.5% AA / 3.2-3.9% Beta

Name: Most
Notes: Most™ hops are a brand new variety that are the collaborative effort of Charles Faram in the UK and farmers in the Czech Republic. Most hops are named after the Czechian city Most in the northwest of the country, which also means bridge. Most hops are seen as a bridge between the UK and EU after the UK departed the union. Most hops are an extremely intense aroma variety that is big with tropical fruits, strawberries, and ruby red grapefruit. Most fits in most fruit-forward styles, but has also performed well in lagers and dry-hopped pilsners. Most Hops Substitutions: 1. BRU-1 hops 2. Belma hops Most Hops Details: Origin: Czech Republic Pedigree: Developed by Charles Faram & Co Ltd by farmers in Czechia. Aromas: Berry & Currant, Tropical Fruit, Citrus Intensity: 8/10 Alpha Acids* 7.0 - 10.0 % Beta Acids 5.0 - 6.5 % Cohumulone 25 - 32 % of alpha acids Total Oil 1.0 - 1.5 ml/100g

Name: Motueka
Notes: Flexible hop developed from Saaz. Has noble character and aroma, lemon and lime tones, balanced bitterness, and unique flavor. Used for: Lagers, Belgian Ales and Bohemian Pilsner. Substitutions: Saaz, Sterling

Name: Moutere
Notes: Formerly called 'Brooklyn'. A high alpha hop with distinctive tropical, grapefruit and passion fruit characters. Developed from Southern Cross. Used for: IPAs Substitutions: N/A

Name: Mt. Hood
Notes: Used for: European Lagers, finishing Aroma: Mild with a clean aroma, neutral flavor. Somewhat pungent. Substitutes: Hallertauer, Liberty, Crystal

Name: Mt. Rainier
Notes: Complex parentage, including Hallertau, Galena, and Fuggle. HopUnion has stated this hop was only bred thru 2010. Used for: Lager Aroma: Excellent floral and noble aromas, with citrus and licorice tones. Substitutions: Hallertau, Fuggle Storage: Unknown 5-8.1% AA / 5-7% Beta

Name: Multihead
Notes: MultiHead is a neomexicanus breed native to New Mexico and is so named because of its tendency to produce dual cones. It is a unique hop in the industry and in brewing features a heady, floral aroma and some citrus elements.

Name: Mystic
Notes: Mystic® Hops are a newer hop variety to be released by the Britain-based Charles Faram Hop Development Programme. Mystic® is a lower alpha acid variety that provides clean bittering in lighter styles, but is mostly used as an aroma variety because of its fruit-forward character. Mystic® is not as big and bold as fellow UK variety Harlequin®, but Mystic® offers a unique combination of black currant, citrus, and passion fruit. Mystic® is the daughter of Jester® and was selected primarily for its amazing aroma, but also because of disease resistance and yield potential. Because Mystic® is such a new variety we are looking to see what you think of it. So be sure to let us know what you brew up with it and how it turns out! Mystic Hop Profile Aroma: Passion fruit, Black currant, Citrus Pedigree: Daughter of Jester® that was selected for its aroma, disease resistance, and yield. So it has a lot going for it. Alpha Acids: 4.0-7.0 % Beta Acids: 2 - 4 % Cohumulone: 30 - 40 % of alpha acids Total Oil: 0.5 - 1.5 ml/100g Myrcene: 30 - 40 % of total oil Humulene: 30 - 40 % of total oil Farnesene: >1 % of total oil Flavor Intensity: 7/10

Name: Nectaron
Notes: A triploid aroma type hop developed in collaboration with New Zealand’s Plant & Food Research. Passionately crafted over 17 years by the hands of New Zealand’s Godfather of Hops – Dr. Ron Beatson. Nectaron® Brand HORT4337 is a full sister to Waimea. Nectaron® provides an intense mix of distinctive pineapple stone fruit citrus that lends itself perfectly to a range of beer styles including NEIPA, IPA, Pale Ales and Lagers. Nectaron is best used late or when dry hopping and has proven itself as an award-winning hop, delivering a consistent dose of high quality tropical hop character every time. Aroma Profile: Citrus, Stone Fruit, Tropical

Name: Nelson Sauvin
Notes: Triploid variety bred from "Smoothcone" released in 2000. Oil profile said to taste like "fresh crushed gooseberries" similar to Sauvignon Blanc, giving it its name. Very fruity, citrusy - use in moderation. Used for: Pale ale, Super Premiums. Substitutes: None

Name: Neo1
Notes: Another neomexicanus breed and native of New Mexico. A high percent of limonene and linalool gives this hop a distinctive strong lemon-citrus character. Sister to Amallia.

Name: Newport
Notes: High alpha hops released in 2002 as a potential replacement for Galena. Note that these hops have a large alpha range of 9.8%-17% so check the package! Fairly pungent. Used for: Primarily used for bittering in ales, stouts, barley wine. Substitutes: Galena

Name: Nobility
Notes: Nobility was developed specifically for lager brewing, with a focus on a balanced noble-style hop character. Primary aromas and flavors of citrus (lemon, orange), lemongrass, green tea, and herbaceous, with a mixture of floral and spice. Nobility Dual-purpose blend for classic lagers from Pils to Bock, as well as hop-forward craft lager styles, Kölsch, Altbier, or any beer that calls for a balanced and refined hop character. Flavor Profile: Citrus, Herbal, Green Tea Beer Style Recommendations: Pilsners, Hoppy Lagers IPL, Kolsch, Altbier.

Name: Northdown
Notes: Variation of Northern Brewers hops grown in England. Used for: Bittering in English ales Aroma: Good bittering, flavor and aroma Substitutes: Northern Brewer

Name: Northern Brewer
Notes: Also called Hallertauer Northern Brewers Used for: Bittering and finishing both ales and lagers of all kinds Aroma: Fine, dry, clean bittering hop. Unique flavor. Substitutes: Hallertauer Mittelfrueh, Hallertauer Examples: Anchor Steam, Old Peculiar,

Name: Nugget
Notes: Used for: Bittering hops for ales Aroma: Strong bittering, heavy, herbal aroma, spicy Substitutes: Chinook Examples: Sierra Nevada Porter

Name: Old Mission
Notes: Old Mission was created as a "field grade" hop by Great Lake Hops (GLH). US hops begin to be harvested in mid-to-late August for most aroma varieties. Aroma/Flavor: Citrus, Pine, Spicy, Tart, Herbal, Black pepper

Name: Olympic
Notes: Olympic is a high alpha hops released by Washington Agricultural Expt Station in 1983. Heritage is from Brewers Gold and Fuggles. Aroma: Spicy, citrus aroma Substitutes: Brewers Gold

Name: Omega
Notes: Omega, despite its pleasant European aroma, decent alpha, storage stability and wilt resistance, exhibited a yield far too low to gain any major foothold commercially. Omega was Dr. R. A. Neve’s swansong before leaving his post in 1984 as head of the Hop Section at the esteemed English horticultural institution, Wye College.

Name: Opal
Notes: New variety from the hop research center in Hull. Used for: Aroma hops in continental styles Aroma: High aroma hops with hints of spice and subtle citrus. Substitutes: Unknown

Name: Orbit
Notes: Selected from a NZ hop breeding program, available in limited quantities. Resinous, piney, aromatic and some notes of tropical fruit. Some variation from year to year due to varieties selected.

Name: Orion
Notes: Cross between Perle and an experimental variety Used for: Bittering and aroma Aroma: Pleasant, hoppy Substitutes: Perle

Name: Outeniqua
Notes: A high alpha bittering hop from South Africa. Primarily a local hop, it features a well balanced oil profile and hoppy, punchy aroma. Mother of the high alpha variety Southern Star.

Name: Pacific Crest Blend
Notes: This blend connects the classic noble varieties with an American influence. Bringing together grassy, earthy and tobacco characteristics with mild floral, spicy, herbal and pine. Pacific Crest works well in Pilsner, Lager and Pale Ales. From Yakima Chief Hops. Aroma Profile: Citrus, Earthy, Grassy, Herbal, Spicy

Name: Pacific Gem
Notes: High alpha smoothcone cross developed at DSIR in 1987. Used for: Bittering - produces a cask-oak woody flavor. Aroma: Pleasant - some blackberry aroma. Substitutes: Bullion

Name: Pacific Hallertau (aka Pacifica)
Notes: Hallertau variant which produces a typical Mittelfruh aroma combined with smooth bouquet and mild buttery flavor notes; typical of the flavor achieved in classic European lagers Used for: Australian Ales, European lager Aroma: Clean, floral with slight cinnamon-like spiciness. Orange marmalade and citrus aroma notes when used in late addition. Soft finish if used in bittering. Substitutes: Chinook, Hallertauer Mittelfruh, Liberty, Mt. Hood Storage: Good/Very Good 5-6% AA / 5.5-6.2% Beta

Name: Pacific Jade
Notes: High alpha bittering hops that delivers a bold herbal infusion of fresh citrus with some black pepper flavor. Citrus aroma and flavor tempers ales and may be used as a finishing hops. Used for: Ales, lagers Substitutes: N/A

Name: Pacific Sunrise
Notes: A bittering hop released by Hort Research in 2000. limited production. A pleasant piney aroma. Cross between a California Late Cluster, Fuggle and New Zealand male plant.

Name: Pacifica
Notes: Aroma hop developed from Hallertauer Mittelfrueh in New Zealand (1994). Blend of old and new world taste including Orange marmalade and citrus aroma notes when used in late addition. Soft finish if used in bittering. Substitutes: Chinook, Hallertauer Mittelfrueh

Name: Palisade
Notes: Also called YCR-4, Palisade is an aroma variety bread by Yakima Chief Ranches. It has both aromatic and moderate bittering properties. Substitutes: Willamette

Name: Paradigm
Notes: ​Paradigm™ is the proud beginning of an entire movement, an industry shift that may very well revitalize what it means to commercially grow hops for Midwest Hop growers. Licensed by Great Lakes Hops. Aroma: Stone fruit, Tropical, Spice, Wild Grape, Melon, Candy, Bright, Herbal

Name: Patagonia Red
Notes: Patagonia Red is grown in Argentina's Patagonia region and the hop cones take on a distinctive red hue. Flavor/Aroma: Spicy, Candy, Strawberry, Sweet, Tropical Fruit, Herbal

Name: Pekko
Notes: Notes: Any style that coud incorporate Saaz could use Pekko. Aroma: Floral, mint, herbal, citrus

Name: Perle
Notes: Perle has a spciy character, and the aroma is moderate and pleasing. Commonly compared to Hallertau Mittlefruh. Used for: German ales, lagers, Plisner, Alt, Maisbier, Dunkle, Kolsch Aroma: Delicate floral and fruity tones Substitutes: Northern Brewer, Perle (U.S.), Hallertau Mittelfruh, Hallertau Aroma Storage: Good 6-10% AA / 2.5-5% Beta

Name: Perle, U.S.
Notes: Bred from English Northern Brewer. Slightly spicy with herbal and floral characteristics. Used for: Stout Substitutions: Northern Brewer, German Northern Brewer, German Perle

Name: Petoskey
Notes: Petoskey™ is possibly an old lost variety that is currently grown by Great Lakes Hops. This is a field grade hop.Petoskey hops have Cluster heritage, but their cones are not shaped like typical Clusters. Aroma: Floral, Pungent, Pineapple

Name: Phoenix
Notes: Seedling derived from Wye Yoeman. Used for: Ales Aroma: Similar to Wye Challenger hops Substitutes: Challenger, East Kent Golding, Northdown

Name: Pilgrim
Notes: New variety from Wye College in 2001. High Selinene content. Used for: Bittering Substitutes: N/A

Name: Pilot
Notes: A very new hops introduced in 2002 by Charles Faram & Co Ltd. Mild herbal flavor with lemon hints and fresh mowed grass flavor. Used for: Primarily for bittering in APAs. Rare.

Name: Pink Boots Blend
Notes: Yakima Chief Hops is proud to partner with the Pink Boots Society for a seventh year on this special seasonal hop blend. We are passionate about promoting diversity in both the hop and beer industries, and hope that our brewing customers will take this opportunity to do the same. We encourage you to get creative and create a tasty brew in celebration of International Women’s Day, and hope this will be a brewing industry tradition for years to come. The 7th Annual blend consists of Ahtanum®, El Dorado®, HBC 638 and Idaho 7®. $3 per pound sold will be donated to Pink Boots Society in support of its mission to provide educational opportunities to women and non-binary individuals in the fermented and alcoholic beverage industry. 2023 - 7th Annual - Ahtanum®, El Dorado®, HBC 638, Idaho 7® 2022 - 6th Annual - Loral®, Ekuanot® , HBC 586 2021 - 5th Annual - HBC 630, Idaho Gem®, Loral®, Talus®, Triumph 2020 - 4th Annual - Ahtanum®, Cashmere, Citra®, Loral®, Sabro® Aroma Profile: Berry, Citrus, Stone Fruit, Tropical

Name: Pioneer
Notes: Bred from Wye Omega at Wye College in the UK Used for: ESB, English Ales Aroma: Mild English hop aroma Substitutes: East Kent Goldings

Name: Polaris
Notes: Very high alpha and stunninly high oil content. Its aroma variously described as "ice candy", eucalyptus, peppermint, and citrus. A variety with bold taste and differentiating flavor. Used for: Lagers, Ales, Pale Ales, IPA Aroma: Intense floral and pleasant mint tones Substitutes: Unknown Storage: Unknown 19-23% AA / 5-7% Beta

Name: Premiant
Notes: A cross between Czech aroma and bittering varieties. Dual purpose though its aroma is not as strong as some other Czech hops. Use in beers where a moderate hoppy aroma is needed. Used for: Moderate ales, lagers Substitutes: Saaz

Name: Pride of Ringwood
Notes: Used for: General purpose bittering hops for Australian beers Aroma: Moderate citric aroma, clean bittering flavor Substitutes: Cluster, Galena

Name: Progress
Notes: Used for: Bittering and aroma in English Ales Aroma: Similar to Fuggles, aromatic, slightly sweet Substitutes: Fuggles

Name: ProvOAK
Notes: Provoak is a mixture of hops and wood that comes in pellet form. It’s made from a proprietary blend of hops and U.S. white oak that has been inspected to the highest food-quality standards. Basically, it’s processed like a normal T-90 or T-45 hop pellet and has similar properties. It’s used for both late hopping and dry-hopping in the same way you would use standard hop pellets.

Name: Puławski
Notes: Introducing Puławski, a captivating newcomer from the IUNG Institute, poised to redefine the landscape of hop-driven aromas. This pungent and complex aroma hop unveils a symphony of scents, featuring the enticing notes of white grapes, a touch of spices, the warmth of clove, and the succulence of ripe papaya. Puławski stands as another beacon in the realm of promising new wave aroma hops. Flavor: Puławski dazzles the palate with a flavor profile that marries pungency and complexity. The dominant notes of white grapes provide a luscious sweetness, while a medley of spices adds layers of intrigue. Clove adds a touch of warmth, and the ripe papaya imparts a tropical twist, creating a sensory experience that transcends expectations. Aroma: Immerse yourself in the rich tapestry of Puławski’s aroma. The fragrant white grapes take center stage, accompanied by a dance of spices that beckon the senses. The subtle warmth of clove and the exotic allure of ripe papaya add nuance, making Puławski a true maestro in the symphony of hop aromas.

Name: PŁ-01
Notes: Embark on a brewing revolution with PŁ-01 – an avant-garde creation from PolishHops that has taken the market by storm. Tailored for the new wave of beer styles, PŁ-01 unveils a symphony of aromas and flavors that redefine the brewing experience. Flavor: PŁ-01 stands as a testament to innovation, boasting a perfect balance of alpha acids at 5%. Dive into a world where lychee, petroleum, pomelo, grapefruit, mandarin, and citrus zest converge to create a flavor profile that transcends tradition. PŁ-01 is your gateway to crafting beers that push the boundaries of taste. Aroma: The aroma of PŁ-01 is a mesmerizing blend of lychee’s sweetness, the intriguing hint of petroleum, and the bright notes of pomelo, grapefruit, mandarin, and citrus zest. This unique combination makes PŁ-01 a sensory journey, inviting brewers to explore uncharted territories of aroma.

Name: Rakau (Alpharoma)
Notes: Previously known as Alpharoma. Suited for new world styles where brash fruit character and big but well constructed bitterness is desired. Has been compared to Nelson Sauvin as a "flavor hop". Used for: Pale Ale, IPA, Lager Aroma: Tropical fruit, passion fruit, apricot/peach Substitutions: Amarillo, Summit Storage: Good 10-11% AA / 4.6-6% Beta

Name: Relax
Notes: From Barth-Haas group - originally bred for tea and not beer. Only 0.25% alpha acid and a huge beta acid punch and high oil content. Use in whirlpool or dry hop for aroma oils. Aromas of alpine meadows, lemongrass, hibiscus, cornflower and honeydew melons.

Name: Riwaka
Notes: Formerly called B-Saaz, this was developed from old line Saazer hops in New Zealand. Used for: New world pale ale, regional pilsners Aroma: Strong, hoppy. Some grapefruit and citrus character.

Name: Rubin
Notes: Rubin is a bitter variety but it is genetically similar to old middle European aroma hops. The Saaz variety evidently influenced its genome. It has fine bitterness with longer dying away in comparison to Saaz.

Name: SAB Blend
Notes: The SAB Blend is a unique hop blend originating from South Africa, designed specifically for dual-purpose use in brewing. This blend is a combination of three distinct hops: Southern Promise at 20%, Southern Star making up the majority at 70%, and Southern Dawn at 10%. While specific flavor profiles for the SAB Blend as a whole are not widely documented, each of its constituent hops brings its own characteristics to the table. Southern Promise is known for its mild and clean bitterness, while Southern Star offers a balanced profile with some berry and pine notes. Southern Dawn, although present in a smaller percentage, can impart a subtle earthiness to the brew.

Name: Saaz
Notes: This traditional variety from the Czech Republic is grown on a limited basis in the U.S. A classic noble aroma that is famously used in Pilsners. Used for: Bohemian-style beers, Continental lagers, Wheats, Pilsener lagers, Ales, Pilsners Aroma: Seasoned herbal character Substitiutions: Czech Saaz, Polish Lublin, Sterling Storage: Poor 2-6% AA / 3-4.5% Beta

Name: Saaz U.S.
Notes: Saaz hops are the famous landrace variety from the Czechia region, but this is specifically US Saaz, grown by our friends at Virgil Gamache Farms in Toppenish, WA. US Saaz caries some of the same floral and spicy elements as Czech Saaz, but this American-grown lot presents with brighter citrus and a hint of cherry. US Saaz hops work well in classic European styles and lagers, but also fits perfectly in new twists on those styles. Aroma: Classic noble aromas. Floral, citrus, and spice. Typical Analytical Range Alpha: 3.0-5.0% Beta: 4.0-6.0% Total Oil: 0.3-1.0 ml/100g US SAAZ HOP SUBSTITUTIONS US Saaz is the obvious choice to replace the scarce Czech grown Saaz. US Saaz hops carry similar characteristics and flavor profiles as East Kent Golding, US Golding, and Hallertau Mittlefrüh making these hops an excellent substitution in your next light lager.

Name: San Juan Ruby Red
Notes: San Juan Ruby Red hops are a captivating find for any homebrewer looking to infuse their beer with a unique American twist. Originating from the rugged terrain of the San Juan Mountains in Colorado, these hops were discovered growing wild, a testament to their hardy nature and the serendipity of open pollination. As an aroma hop, San Juan Ruby Red is prized for its contribution to the olfactory experience of beer brewing. It imparts a fruity and slightly sweet aroma that can elevate the sensory profile of a brew. The noble hop characteristics it possesses suggest a refined bitterness and a delicate, herbaceous quality, making it an excellent choice for brewers aiming to craft beers with a sophisticated bouquet and a balanced flavor profile. Its use is particularly well-suited to styles that benefit from a subtle hop presence and a nuanced aroma, such as pale ales, IPAs, or even lagers that call for a touch of fruitiness without overpowering the palate. Flavor/Aroma: Fruity, Herbal, Noble, Sweet, Delicate

Name: Santiam
Notes: Triploid aroma selection derived from Tettnang and Hallertauer (1997) Used for: Lager, U.S. Pale Ale, Pilsner Substitutes: German Tettnang, Spalt, Spalt Select

Name: Saphir
Notes: A new noble breed as an alternative to Hallertau Mittelfrueh in an attempt to make it more disease resistant and commercially viable. Similar in character to Hallertau varieties. Used for: Pilsners, lagers, Belgian whites. Substitutes: Hallertau Mittelfrueh

Name: Sasquatch
Notes: Sasquatch® is a wild hop variety that was discovered in the mountain region of British Columbia. It is grown exclusively by our Canadian sister company Hops Connect at two farms: Leamington, Ontario and Chilliwack, BC. Sasquatch is the first Canadian hop variety that is proprietary and patented. Sasquatch offers subtle and delicate flavors of citrus and sweet fruit as well as a soothing blend of hay, sweet cream, and rose. Profile: Floral, Lemon, Tangerine

Name: Satus
Notes: High alpha bittering hops from Yakima that is similar to Galena. Of recent origin. Substitutes: Galena

Name: Savinjski Golding
Notes: Originating from UK Fuggle, Styrian Savinjski Golding is a traditional Slovenian variety with pleasant bittering and noble aroma characteristics. Aroma Profile: Citrus, Earthy, Floral, Spicy

Name: Select
Notes: An aroma hop known for its spicy flavor profile that adds complex depth of flavor to any beer. Aroma: Spicy - floral, grassy, herbal, resinous, cucumber, lemon, citrusy, fruity, caramel, woody, curant Alpha Acid: 3.0 - 6.5% Beta Acid: 2.5 - 5.0% Total Oil: 0.5 - 0.9 ml/100g

Name: Select Spalt
Notes: Bred from Hallertauer Mittelfrueh and Spalt in Germany. Spalt character. Used for: Lager, Pilsner, Noble aroma hop substitute Aroma: Very fine, spalt aroma Substitutes: Spalter, Saaz, Tettnang

Name: Sequoia
Notes: HS Sequoia® is a hop blend from the BSG Hop Solutions program, formerly known as HS-1228, available exclusively from BSG. Its sensory profile is a mix of tropical fruit and classic West Coast hop character. HS Sequoia® is designed as a late kettle or dry hop for classic West Coast-inspired IPAs showcasing notes of tropical fruit, citrus, and pine. Its strong tropical fruit expression also makes it well-suited to hazy or juicy IPA, as well as other hop-forward styles. Aroma/Flavor: tropical fruit (mango, pineapple) with citrus and pine.

Name: Serebrianka (Silver Hops)
Notes: A Russian aroma hop that is actually a parent of Cascade. Not widely grown in the US, but still available in some areas. Hints of black tea, herbs, tobacco with high humulene and fanesene content that contributes to pleasant continental aroma and taste.

Name: Shinshuwase
Notes: Grown exclusively in Japan, Shinshuwase is a high yielding aroma hop. It dates back to the early 1900’s, possibly earlier. While it still exists, it has largely been replaced by Kirin II, Toyomidori, Kitamidori and Eastern Gold as a commercial crop. A cross between Saaz and open pollinated White Vine, Shinshuwase was originally used as a bittering hop, but after the introduction of super alpha varieties, it is now considered far more useful as an aroma hop. Shiga Kogen Beer currently uses it in that capacity for their Draft Pale Ale and Miyama Blonde and describes its aroma as lemon-centric.

Name: Simcoe
Notes: High alpha bittering hops with good aroma characteristics. Used for: IPAs Aroma: Citrus and pine-like aroma Substitutes: N/A

Name: Sladek
Notes: Hybrid variety of Saaz origin with noble hoppy taste and aroma. Generally recommended for middle-hopping about 20 min before end of boil. Not a replacement for Saaz, but complimentary in Lagers. Used for: Lager Substitutes: Saaz

Name: Smaragd
Notes: Smaragd is another interesting hop to come out of the Hop Research Institute in Hüll, Germany. Bred with the purpose of creating a more disease resistant alternative to Hallertau Mittelfrüh, it was released for large-scale production in 2007. Meaning ‘emerald’ in German, Smaragd is a low acid, low cohumulone aroma hop with an interesting oil balance. Myrcene and farnesene make up the majority of its oil composition. Its aroma is predominantly fruity but with some floral elements as well. Its flavor profile has been described as spicy. Daughter of Hallertau Gold, Smaragd is a wonderful dual-purpose hop. Aroma: Predominantly fruity aroma with bold floral notes, flavors of earth and spice

Name: Smaragd (Emerald)
Notes: Perviously known as Emerald. A fine aroma variety with high bittering value. Used for: Alt, Kolsch, or blond ale. Aroma: Fine aroma, floral, fruity, spicy Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Fair 4-6% AA / 3.5-5.5% Beta

Name: Sonnet Golding
Notes: Sonnet is a new hop variety bred from a Saaz variant that exhibited Goldings-like characteristics. It's used for aroma due to its low alpha acid content, and offers lots of floral notes. Used for: English Ales, Belgian Style Ales Substitutions: Saaz,East Kent Golding and Crystal. Storage: Unknown

Name: Sorachi Ace
Notes: Developed by Sapporo Breweries and used in their beers - has a Brewer's gold and Saaz heritage. Works well with Citra and Simcoe. Used for: Saison, IPA, Pale Ale, Wheat, or other beer where lemon/citrus is desirable. Aroma: Striking lemon charachter, a bold hop for bold beers. Substitutes: Admiral, Cascade Storage: Unknown 10-16% AA / 6-7% Beta

Name: Southern Aroma
Notes: The largest commercial brewery in South Africa uses Southern Aroma, a relatively new South African hop varietal. Southern Aroma is daylight hop with a diploid seed derived from a hybrid between Hallertauer Mittelfrueh and Saaz. Note that Motueka is a possible comparable hop. SA Genetic Origin: Saaz & Hallertau Mittelfruher SA Aroma Notes: flowery fields, dried herbs, parsley, grain, straw, sunny hayfield, woody

Name: Southern Brewer
Notes: Developed in the early 1970's to grow in South Africa after Golden Cluster was devistated by black root. Primarily a bittering hop, not known for its aroma or unusual flavors. Did not grow as well in limited sunlight but was bread to create Southern Promise.

Name: Southern Cross
Notes: Dual purpose with "European" flavor - from DSIR in 1994 Used for: European flavor bittering/aroma Aroma: Spicy, lemony, slight pine and wood flavors Substitutes: N/A

Name: Southern Dawn
Notes: A relatively new South African hop used by some large breweries. Resembles Southern Promise but with a different aroma profile. Has a fruity, spicy aroma and is relatively neutral when bittering.

Name: Southern Passion
Notes: Southern Passion is a South African hop variety that has its genetic roots in Europe. Southern Passion is the resulting cross of Czech Saaz and German Hallertauer. Both unique and complex, Southern Passion offers ripe tropical fruit flavors like passion fruit, guava, and coconut as well as citrus and red berries. Some brewers describe a sunny floral fragrance that can work well in lagers, wits, and Belgian ales. Alpha Acids: 5 – 12% Beta Acids: 4 – 6% Cohumulone: 16.6 – 20.2% Total Oil (ml/100g): 0.7 – 1.3% Humulene: 17 – 35% Caryophyllene: 8 – 13% Farnesene: 1 – 3% Linalool: <1% Geraniol: 0.5%

Name: Southern Promise
Notes: A high alpha hop bred in South Africa. Created from Southern Brewer and a wild Slovenian male hop, it was bred to grow in limited sunlight. Has a high alpha acid and cohumulone content giving it bright, smooth bittering qualities. Can be dual-use with woody and earthy qualities.

Name: Southern Star
Notes: A high alpha hop from South Africa released in 2001. Used primarily for bittering but features a spicy, tangy flavor and some aroma.

Name: Southern Sublime
Notes: Southern Sublime (N1/69) has a Kool-Aid, citrus, mango, juicy fruit, dank, plums scent profile. It also boasts a delicious terroir backdrop and a traditional noble hop flavor.

Name: Southern Tropic
Notes: Southern Tropic is a South African high alpha hop variety. Southern Tropic previously went by the breeding number U3/108 and is the offspring of USDA650-09 and XJA2/574. It offers big aromas across a wide spectrum, including guava, mango, and lychee with plenty of pine, resin, and lemongrass. There's even a hint of coconut or bubblegum that offers a unique splash in any style. Southern Tropic hops work well in any fruit-forward IPA or Hazy IPA where you are going after sweet, tropical fruit. Its resinous elements could also work their way into a West Coast IPA or classic American Pale Ale. Southern Tropic Hop Analysis Alpha Acids: 17 – 18% Beta Acids: 6.4 – 6.6% Cohumulone: 26 – 28% Total Oils: 1.3 ml/100g Farnesene: 9 – 10.25% Linalool: 0.1 - 0.4 %

Name: Sovereign
Notes: Pleasant but intense fruity flavors with mild floral, grassy, herbal and mint characteristics. Used for: Pale Ale Substitution: Fuggles Storage: Unknown

Name: Spalt Spalter
Notes: Available only from the Spalt region. fire aroma is unique despite genetic sililarities to Saaz, Tettnanger. Used for: Traditional German beers, Altbiers, Lagers Aroma: Fine/spicy, delicate, herbal, woody, floral Substitutes: Saaz, Tettnanger Storage: Poor 2.5-3.5 AA / 3-5% Beta

Name: Spalter
Notes: Used for: Traditional German bittering and aroma hops, Altbiers, Lagers Aroma: Mild, pleasant, slight spice Substitutes: Saaz, Tettnang Examples: Dusseldorf Altbiers

Name: Spalter Select
Notes: Bred from Hallertauer Mittelfrueh and Spalt in Germany. Solid replacement for Sazzer-type hops. Used for: Lager, Pilsner Aroma: Spicy, floral, and woody. Substitutes: Spalt Spalter, Saaz, Tettnanger Storage: Good/Fair 3-6.5% AA / 2.5-5% Beta

Name: Sterling
Notes: Flavor is a cross between Saaz and Mt Hood Used for: Lagers, Ales, Pilsners Aroma: Herbal, spicy, some floral and citrus hints Substitutes: Saaz, Polish Lublin

Name: Sticklebract
Notes: High alpha hops from DSIR research first released in 1972. Some of the same qualities as Northern Brewer Used for: Dual purpose Aroma: Pine-like with a hint of citrus Substitutes: Northern Brewer

Name: Strisslespalt
Notes: Aroma hops used widely in the Alsace area of France. Used for: Pilsner, lager, wheat Aroma: Medium intensity, pleasant, hoppy Substitutes: Mt Hood, Crystal, Hallertauer Hersbruck

Name: Styrian Aurora (Super Styrian)
Notes: Typical alpha: 7.0-9.0%. Moderate-alpha with powerful hoppy aroma, very versatile - try in US, Belgian, or English ales. Uses: All Ales, some lagers Aroma: Spicy, yet pleasant hoppiness Substitutes: Styrian Golding, Styrian Bobek Storage: Excellent 6.5-9.5% AA / 2.7-5.5% Beta

Name: Styrian Bobek
Notes: Derived from a Northern Brewer and Yugoslavian male TG. Low to moderate alpha and earthy, slightly fruity aroma. Uses: English Style Ales, ESB, Lager, Pilsner & Belgian-style Ales Aroma: Intense and pleasant hoppy aroma Substitutions: Styrian Golding, UK or US Fuggle, Willamette Storage: Good 3.5-7% AA / 4-6.1% Beta

Name: Styrian Cardinal
Notes: Cardinal was developed and released by the Slovenian Institute of Hop Research and Brewing. Cardinal was bred from European and American germplasm. Sometimes referred to as just Cardinal. Flavor/Aroma: Anise, Apple, Citrus, Gooseberry, Fruity, Pineapple, Tropical Fruits, Strawberry

Name: Styrian Celeja
Notes: Hybrid cross between Styrian Golding, Styrian Aurora, and Slovenian wild hops. Aroma is orange marmalade like Styrian Golding, but with a noble component. Used for: UK and Belgian ales or lagers. Aroma: Floral, fruit Substitutions: Unknown (Styrian Golding?) Storage: Unknown 3-6% AA / ??% Beta

Name: Styrian Dragon
Notes: Styrian Dragon is a type of hop known for its intense aroma and high alpha acid content, making it a popular choice for brewing hop-forward beers. This hop variety was bred at the Slovenian Institute of Hop Research and Brewing in Žalec from European and American germplasm. Styrian Dragon hops are known for their unique blend of spicy, herbal, and fruity flavors, which make them a versatile choice for brewing a wide range of beer styles. They have a high alpha acid content, which means they contribute bitterness to the beer, making them ideal for use in IPAs and other hop-forward styles. They also have a high oil content, which contributes to their intense aroma and flavor profile. Styrian Dragon hops are typically used in the late additions of the brewing process, either as a single-hop variety or as part of a blend with other hop varieties. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Grapefuit, Lime, Orange

Name: Styrian Golding (Savinja Golding)
Notes: A seedless version of Fuggle grown in Slovenia. Popular in Europe and the United Kingdom. Used for: All Ales and Lagers Aroma: Delicate, slightly spicy, soft, and floral aroma. Similar to Fuggle. Substitutes: Fuggle, Williamette, EKG Storage: Very Good 4.5-6% AA / 2-3.5% Beta

Name: Styrian Goldings
Notes: A seedless version of Fuggles grown in Slovenia Used for: Bittering, finishing for a wide variety of European Beers. Popular in Europe and the United Kingdom Aroma: Mild, soft, grassy, floral aroma. Similar to Fuggles. Substitutes: Fuggles, Williamette

Name: Styrian Kolibri
Notes: Developed and released by the Slovenian Institute of Hop Research and Brewing, from European and American germplasm. When Styrian Kolibri was used by craft brewers, they have noticed very attractive floral notes in a range of beer varieties.

Name: Sultana
Notes: From the Hopsteiner Breeding Program. An aroma hop known for its big cone size, big oils, and big aromas suitable for a variety of different beer styles. Aroma: Citrus - pineapple, stone fruit, berries, citrus, pine Alpha Acid: 12.0 - 15.0% Beta Acid: 4.0 - 5.0% Total Oil: 2.5 - 4.0 ml/100g

Name: Summer (Summer Saaz)
Notes: Apricot and melon characters are more apparent when dry hopping. Used for: Ales Aroma: Subtle, light apricot and melon flavors Substitiutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 4-7.5% AA / 4.8-6.7% Beta

Name: Summit
Notes: High alpha variety bred by American Dwarf Hop Assoc. Can be grown commercially on a low trellis. Aroma: Strong citrus, grapefruit notes. Substitutes: Warrior, Millenium, Columbus, Tomahawk, Zeus

Name: Summit Hop Extract
Notes: CO2 Hop Extract -sold in both 0.5kg (150 gma), 1 kg (300 gma), 2 kg, 3kg and 4kg cans. Extracted from Summit hops and only for use in the boil. Measure out liquid in ml for use. Check alpha percentage listed here against the can as alpha may vary by season and manufacturer.

Name: Sun
Notes: A very high alpha bittering hops from Yakima Valley, Washington Used for: Bittering (intense) Aroma: Pleasant, hoppy Substitutes: Other very high alpha US hops

Name: Sunbeam
Notes: Not originally envisioned for use in brewing, Sunbeam is an ornamental hop that was discovered to be inadvertently useful as an aroma hop. Selected in 1992 in Corvallis, Oregon, it features characteristics similar to that of its parent variety, Saaz.

Name: Sunshine
Notes: Mother to Comet, Sunshine was developed via open pollination seedling selection at Wye College in England. It was selected by Professor E. R. Salmon in the early 1930’s and exhibits a very similar oil profile to Comet. While it is considered a brewing hop first and foremost, its lovely, lemon yellow colored leaves also give this variety ornamental potential. Like Sunbeam hops, it should to be grown in a shaded position or it risks sustaining necrotic burn when exposed to prolonged late summer sun. Its exact parentage is unknown.

Name: Super Alpha
Notes: High alpha hops released in 1976 by DSIR. European in character - clean crisp flavor and resin aroma. Used for: European Styles, General purpose bittering Aroma: Pleasant, resin like aroma Substitutes: N/A

Name: Super Galena
Notes: Super high alpha hops from Hopsteiner breeding program released in 2006. High alpha and beta content - comparable to Galena in bitterness and aroma profile. Used for: Primarily for bitterness. Substitutes: Galena

Name: Super Pride
Notes: A high alpha variation of the Pride of Ringwood hop variety. Features mild aromas, higher alpha content and low cohumulone levels. Used for: Primarily for bittering. Substitutes: Pride of Ringwood

Name: Superdelic
Notes: Superdelic™ is a triploid variety (cultivar) that was crossed by Dr. Ron Beatson in 2012 through our partnership breeding program with Plant and Food Research (PFR). Superdelic was developed from both European and North American ancestry. After showing promise in clonal selection trials, and showing sensory attributes of berry fruit in pilot brewing trials, Superdelic was fast-tracked into the NZ Hops trial hop farm program in 2017, due to its unique aroma and chemistry profile. Aroma Profile: Berry, Citrus, Sweet Aromatic, Tropical

Name: Sussex
Notes: Aroma similar to Fuggle. Good, yet delicate, flavor retention. Pedigree is "a chance find". Used for: Ale Sustitution: N/A Storage: Unknown

Name: Sybilla
Notes: A varity from Poland which is primarily for bittering but has some good aromatic properties. A mix of Lublin and Slovenian Styrian Goldings. Good aroma hop with soft contintental character similar to a German Hallertauer, English Fuggles or Sterling.

Name: Sylva
Notes: Formerly Southern Saaz. Displays what's considered the traditional hoppy (with cedarwood notes) and spicy character of Saaz. Mostly an option for Southern Hemisphere brewers but an interesting variation on Saaz. Used for: Lagers Aroma: Complex and subtle, floral and herbal characteristics Substitutions: Hallertau Mittlefrüh, AU Helga Storage: Unknown 4-7.3% AA / 3-5% Beta

Name: TNT Blend
Notes: TNT Blend is a product of both US and German cultivation and not just a single hop, but a blend of several varietals. It is renowned for its unique aroma profile, which is often described as a fruity explosion on the palate. Specifically, the T'N'T blend is a harmonious combination of select German hop varieties, meticulously crafted to emphasize the flavors of red fruits, notably pomegranate, sweet fruits, and citrus. This results in a beer that boasts a robust tropical foundation. When used in brewing, the TNT Blend is typically incorporated during the late boil stages, including the dry hopping process. This hop blend is designed to impart a memorable and refreshing taste, making it a favorite among brewers aiming for a tropical hop character in their brews. Aroma/Flavor: Berries, Caramel, Citrus, Creamy, Fruity, Spicy, Sweet, Strawberry, Tropical Fruits

Name: Tahoma
Notes: Citrus and lemon notes with cedar, pine, floral, pepper and subtle green melon notes. Daughter of Glacier that retains the very low cohumulone characteristic of Glacier with somewhat higher alpha acid content.

Name: Taiheke
Notes: Formerly called 'Cascade, New Zealand'. Flowers, citrus & spice with grapefruit the noticeable fragrance quite often. This medium aroma balances the low bittering value. Very popular hop among craft brewers. Used for: Pale Ales, IPAs, Porters Substitutions: Centennial, Amarillo

Name: Talus
Notes: Talus ® delivers big aromas of pink grapefruit, citrus rinds, dried roses, pine resin, tropical fruits and sage. These unique and impactful aromas remain throughout the brewing process. With Talus, what you smell in the field is what you’ll get in your glass. Aroma Profile: Grapefruit, Herbal, Pine, Rose, Tropical

Name: Tango
Notes: Tango hops, a modern marvel in the world of brewing, are a testament to the innovative spirit of hop breeders. Originating from the Hüll Hop Research Institute in the Hallertau region of Germany, Tango is a product of the union between the globally recognized Cascade and a male Hüll breeding line. This hop variety was developed as a response to the challenges posed by climate change and the increasing emphasis on environmental protection. As a result, Tango stands out as a climate-tolerant variety that offers excellent brewing quality, making it a sustainable alternative to traditional aroma varieties. The flavor profile of Tango hops is both diverse and captivating. At its core, it carries classically hoppy notes, which are complemented by a delightful fruity aroma. When used early in the brewing process, Tango ensures a stable hop note, characterized by its pleasantly hoppy, fresh undertones with hints of citrus and green fruits. However, when added late or used for cold hopping, Tango unveils an impressively fruity aroma spectrum, dominated by passion fruit notes. This fruity character can be further amplified when brewed with aroma-intensive top-fermenting yeasts or ale yeasts. Aroma/Flavor: Apple, Cassis, Citrus, Earthy, Grapefruit, Lemon, Passion Fruit, Pineapple, Resinous, Spicy

Name: Tardif de Bourgogne
Notes: "Late Burgundy" hops grown in small quantities in Alsace, France. A mild European style aroma. Very limited quantities commercially.

Name: Target
Notes: Used for: English ales and lagers Aroma: Robust aroma and flavor Substitutes: East Kent Goldings

Name: Teamaker
Notes: Developed by the USDA, Teamaker was released in the late 2000's. It is a unique high beta, very low alpha breed sued in medicine and food industries as well as brewing. It has very strong antibiotic properrties, and makes a tasty iced tea. It imparts almost no bittering content, but has floral aromas. Use as a late addition either whirlpool or dry hopping.

Name: Tettnang
Notes: Used for: German ales, lagers and wheat beer Aroma: Noble, mild, fine, slightly spicy Substitutes: Saaz, Spalt Examples: Sam Adams Octoberfest, Anderson Valley ESB

Name: Tettnang (Tettnang Tettnager)
Notes: One of the four noble hops along with Saaz, Spalt, and Hallertau Mittelfrueh, a group of mainland European hops that are prized for their similar, delicate flavor and aroma profile. Used for: German ales, lagers, wheat Aroma: Noble, Mild and pleasant with balanced earthy, herbal and floral aroma impressions Substitutes: Saaz, Spalt, Hallertau Mittelfrueh Storage: Poor 2.5-6% AA / 3-5% Beta

Name: Tettnang, U.S.
Notes: Tettnang is a traditional German land-race variety known for its noble aroma that is pleasant and slightly spicy. It remains well established in the Tettnang growing region of Germany, and is also grown in Oregon and Washington. Americangrown Tettnang is reported to have slightly higher myrcene levels than its German counterpart. Used for: American style ale Substitutions: German Tettnang, Santiam, German Spalt, Spalt Select

Name: Tettnanger
Notes: An all-purpose hop known for its ability to provide spicy, floral aroma with great bitterness. Aroma: Spicy - floral, pepper, black tea, resinous, cucumber, grassy, currant Alpha Acid: 2.5 - 5.5% Beta Acid: 3.0 - 5.0% Total Oil: 0.4 - 0.9 ml/100g

Name: The Bruce
Notes: The Bruce is an experimental blend put together by Freestyle Hops in New Zealand. The blend is made up of Nelson Sauvin™, early season Motueka™, late season Pacifica™, and mid season Pacific Jade™. The blend is named after Bruce Eggers, a farmer of hops in Sunrise Valley that has been helpful by sharing is hop knowledge. Flavor/Aroma: Gooseberry, Citrus Peel, Tropical Fruit, Orange Blossom

Name: Tillicum
Notes: High alpha and high beta hops developed though Haas breeding program and released in 1995. Daughter variety of Galena and related to Chelan - so similar to both. Substitutes: Galena, Chelan

Name: Tomahawk
Notes: Part of the Columbus/Tomahak/Zeus (CTZ) group of super high alpha hops. Together these make up 1/4 of US hop acreage. Used for: High alpha hops used primarily for commercial bittering. Substitutes: Tomahawk, Zeus, Nugget, Galena, Chinook

Name: Tomyski
Notes: Enter the realm of legend with Tomyski, an aromatic hop that, for years, remained absent from the brewing scene. Thanks to the efforts of PolishHops, this variety has been resurrected and brought back to life. Tomyski, also known as Nowotomyski, is a hop with historical roots, named after the town of Nowy Tomyśl in western Poland where it was originally cultivated. This hop, once instrumental in brewing the iconic Grodziskie beer, is now ready to weave its aromatic tales once again. Flavor: Tomyski, a hop shrouded in legend, unfolds its aromatic prowess with a flavor profile deeply rooted in history. Used historically in brewing Grodziskie beer, it imparts a gentle touch of bitterness with a captivating aroma. With alpha acids ranging from 2.5% to 4%, Tomyski invites brewers to explore the nuances of its subtle bitterness and aromatic charm. Aroma: Experience the resurgence of Tomyski’s aroma – a symphony of myrcene, humulene, and caryophyllene. The subtle notes of lime and citrus, expressed through limonene, add a refreshing twist to this historical hop. Tomyski’s aroma is a bridge between brewing traditions and contemporary creativity.

Name: Topaz
Notes: Very high alpha hop variety from Australia. Used for commercial production primarily for its high alpha acid content and high bitterness.

Name: Tradition
Notes: Close descendant of Hallertauer Mittelfrueh bred for high yield. Used for: Lager, Pilsner, Bock, some Weizens Aroma: Fine - similar to Hallertauer Mittelfrueh Substitutes: Hallertauer Mittelfrueh, Liberty, Ultra

Name: Traful
Notes: Traful hops are a testament to the ingenuity and dedication of the Argentinian brewing industry, particularly the efforts of the Quilmes Brewing and Malting Company. Developed through a breeding program that began in the 1980s, Traful hops are a product of a cross between the popular Cascade variety and Quilmes' proprietary lines. Officially registered in 1994, these hops were initially cultivated for their aromatic qualities. It wasn't until the last decade, however, that their unique flavor profile began to be embraced by craft brewers, particularly for Ale and Belgian-style beers. With a growing area that is slowly expanding, Traful hops have become a sought-after variety for those looking to impart a distinctive Argentinian touch to their brews. The flavor profile of Traful hops is intriguingly complex, marked by a spicy and vegetal character with subtle fruity undertones. Brewers have noted the presence of black pepper, fennel, anise, and green tea notes, with hints of grape and fig adding to the hop's layered aroma. Despite its low total oil content, Traful is rich in linalool, a compound that contributes to its potent aroma and flavor, especially when added late in the brewing process or during the whirlpool. This makes it an excellent choice for brewers looking to experiment with flavor without overwhelming their beer. They are particularly well-suited to Pale Ales, Pilsners, Lagers, and Belgian styles such as Dubbel and Tripel. Flavor/Aroma: Anise, Black Pepper, Fig, Fruity, Green Tea, Spicy, Vegetal

Name: Triple Pearl
Notes: Pleasant and balanced with notes of melon, orange citrus, resin, spice and pepper. Stoarge stability unknown. Released in 2013 by USDA-ARS. Used for: Pale Ale Substitutions: Perle

Name: Triskel
Notes: Triskel was developed in France in 2006. It is a cross from Strisselspalt and Yoeman from England. It is often used in Belgian ales and featured as a late, whirlpool or dry hop. It has floral and citrus notes with a fruity finish. Comes from Alsace.

Name: Triumph
Notes: Developed by John Hennings, a research geneticist at the USDA, Triumph is an aroma hop produced by crossing Nugget with USDA 21110M. With high yields and excellent brewing quality, this public variety was named for its triumph over other experimental hop varieties both in the field and in the glass – as well as Henning’s love of Triumph brand motorcycles. Aroma Profile: Bubblegum, Citrus, Herbal, Tropical

Name: Tropica
Notes: Grown from a Chinook by Mighty Axe Hops. Mighty Axe’s custom growing practices create Tropica’s distinct tropical character. Flavor/Aroma: Mandarin, Orange, Pineapple, Tropical Fruits

Name: Tsingtao Flower
Notes: Accounts for about 65% of acreage in China, the Chinese version of American Cluster. Used for: Unknown (Lager?) Aroma: Floral and Spicy Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Good 6-8% AA / 3-4.2% Beta

Name: Ultra
Notes: Triploid seedling of the German Hallertauer Variety Used for: Lager, Pilsner, wheat, finishing hops in ales Aroma: Good to outstanding - some Saaz like qualities Substitutes: Liberty, Hallertauer, Tradition, Saaz

Name: Vanguard
Notes: Diploid seedling made in 1982, released in 1997. Hallertau Mitterfrueh parentage. Used for: European lagers, ales Aroma: Strong aromatic notes Substitutes: Hallertauer Mittelfrueh

Name: Vermelho
Notes: Vermelho is a unique Polish new wave hop that has, intriguingly, been primarily available in Brazil. Now, these hops are making their way to Poland, exclusively accessible through homebrewing.pl, offering Polish brewers a taste of this distinctive variety. With a high alpha acid content, Vermelho hops are known for their robust bittering potential, which is a key attribute to craft beers with a solid hop backbone. The flavor profile of Vermelho is particularly notable for its intense fruity character, with a pronounced emphasis on red fruit notes. This makes it a great choice for brewers who want to infuse their beers with a rich, fruit-forward complexity. Aroma/Flavor: Aromatic, Berries, Fruity, Intense

Name: Veterans Blend
Notes: From Yakima Chief Hops. The 6th annual blend is comprised of four different Pacific Northwest-grown varieties including Citra®, HBC 638, Talus® and Sabro® brand hops. It will perform well in any hop forward beer, providing a well-balanced mix of tropical, citrus, and sweet aromatic aromas. The blend creates a hop pellet with a much wider range of flavor characteristics than could be provided by any one variety alone. Yakima Chief Hops is donating $3 per pound sold to Homes For Our Troops, a national nonprofit organization that builds and donates specially adapted custom homes nationwide for severely injured post-9/11 Veterans. hfotusa.org. 2023 - 6th Annual Veterans Blend - Citra®, HBC 638, Talus®, Sabro® 2022 - 5th Annual Veterans Blend - HBC 586, Ekuanot®, Idaho 7®, Chinook, Triumph 2021 - 4th Annual Veterans Blend - Azacca®, Ekuanot®, HBC 472, HBC 630, Idaho 7® 2020 - 3rd Annual Veterans Blend - HBC 630, Talus™, Idaho 7 ®, Mosaic®, Sabro® Aroma Profile: Citrus, Sweet Aromatic, Tropical

Name: Vic Secret
Notes: The clean, distinct fruit and pine characters of Vic Secret are best accessed by dry hopping or whirlpool additions. Late kettle additions have been found to impart a pleasant earthy character, but little fruit. Maintains very high levels of alpha acid so brewers may find it easier to craft the right balance in their beer by dry hopping. First commercial harvest in 2013. Uses: Ales, Lagers, Experimental Ales Aroma: Clean, distinct fruit and pine Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 14-17% AA / 2.2-2.8% Beta

Name: Victoria
Notes: Hi alpha hops grown in Victoria, Australia. It was created in 1976 and entered commercial production in the 1990s. Used for: Primarily for bittering due to high alpha content.

Name: Viking
Notes: Developed in 1968 at Wye College in England. A cross between Svalof and an English male plant. High alpha for a British hop - the aroma is described as pleasant and hoppy.

Name: Vinnie Special
Notes: Originating from an undisclosed lineage, its aromatic characteristics are predominantly citrusy, with hints of stone fruit, tropical fruit, and herbal undertones. Beer enthusiasts who have had the pleasure of tasting beers brewed with this hop variety indicates a flavor profile that leans heavily towards sweet orange, albeit not as intense as some might prefer. The aroma, while delightful, isn't overpoweringly loud. The Vinnie Special presents an opportunity to experiment and diversify their brewing repertoire. Its smooth bitterness makes it an ideal candidate for early boil additions to enhance the bitterness of the beer. However, its subtle nature means it can be complemented with later additions of spicier or floral hops to achieve a more balanced and layered flavor profile. Given its strong citrus element, it's well-suited for styles like Saison, Witbier, and Belgian Pale Ale. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Fruity, Herbal, Mango, Orange, Pine, Stone Fruit, Tropical Fruits

Name: Vista
Notes: Looking for more info about using Vista hops? Previously named USDA Elite Line 074, Vista Hops can add a new flavor profile to pilsners and lagers as well as new elements to your IPA. They are the newest variety to be released by the USDA public breeding program. Vista emerged from the Elite Line because of brewers' feedback at the 2019 Hopsource that was put on by the Brewer's Association and hosted here at Yakima Valley Hops. Brewers who participated in the blind smell tests described Vista as having Stone Fruit, Citrus, Tropical Fruit, and Floral characteristics. It ranked highly on the hedonic score with most brewers "Liking it Very Much" or "Liking it Moderately". Vista also ranked 7/10 on the potency scale so it can excel in fruit-forward IPAs and Pale Ales, but brewers also said it would work well in lighter, more delicate styles like lagers and pilsners. Vista Aroma: Tropical Fruit, Tangerine, Melon, Pear, Green Tea Vista Typical Analytical Range Alpha: 11 - 12 % Beta: 4 - 5 % Total Oil: 1.0-2.0 mL/100g

Name: Vital
Notes: A high alpha hop from the Zatac breeding program. High alpha and beta content provide a spicy aroma. Used in some single hop pale ales with success.

Name: Vojvodina
Notes: A cross between Northern Brewer and a Golding male. Mild, woody aroma with hints of tobacco and woody aroma. Some noble character but more rounded. Somewhat scarce availability.

Name: Wai-iti
Notes: Strong citrus character of lime zest with fresh peaches and apricot. Used in both ales and lagers. Good for late additions and dry hopping. Used for: Ale, Pale Ale, IPA. Substitutions: N/A

Name: Waimea
Notes: A high alpha acid, dual purpose hop with lots of pine and citrus characters. Used for: IPA Substitutions: N/A

Name: Wakatu (Hallertau Aroma)
Notes: Formerly called Hallertau Aroma. Similar to German Hallertau but higher alpha. Excellent Dual Purpose hop, rounded floral aroma with hints of lime. Used for: Lager, Pils, Bock, Wheat, Kolsch, Helles, Belgian Ales. Aroma: Restrained floral notes and freshly zested lime Substitutions: US Hallertau, Liberty Storage: Good 6.5-8.5% AA / 8-9% Beta

Name: Warrior
Notes: High alpha hops from Yakima Chief Ranches. Also called YCR-5. Mild aroma and low cohumulone content - primarily for bittering. Used for: Ales, stouts Aroma: Neutral, clean, mild Substitutes: Nugget, Columbus, Magnum

Name: Whitbread Golding Variety (WGV)
Notes: Derived in England in 1911 from Bates Brewer. Some Golding characteristics, but lacking the full Goldings aroma. Used for: Ales Aroma: Pleasant, hoppy, slightly intense Substitutes: East Kent Goldings, Progress

Name: Willamette
Notes: Used for: Finishing American and British Ales Aroma: Mild, grassy, floral, slightly spicy Substitutes: Fuggles, East Kent Goldings Examples: Sierra Nevada Porter, Ballard Bitter

Name: Willamette, New Zealand
Notes: Mild, pleasant, slight spicy floral tones. Used for: American and English Ales Substitutions: US Willamette, Fuggles

Name: Willow Creek
Notes: A collected wild neo, Mexicana species from Colorado. It displays better tolerance to cold and moisture than other neo. Mex. types. It has large cones with the typical high cohumulone levels of the species and strong citrus aromas. Compare to Amallia, Neo 1, and Multihead. Grows best at mid to lower latitudes. Early to mid-season maturity. Water and fertilizer should be metered over time in smaller quantities than most commercial hops. Noted Aromas: Mild with pleasant notes of Pineapple

Name: Wye Challenger
Notes: Spicy, clean, citrus aroma with distinct herbal notes. Used for: English Ales, English IPA, Red Ale, Bitter, Stout, Porter Substitutions: N/A

Name: Yakima Cluster
Notes: The Yakima Cluster hop, with its rich lineage, has its roots in the United States. It is the offspring of the Late Cluster hop and the grandchild of the Pacific Coast Cluster. This hop variety first made its appearance in the US in 1950's. Interestingly, while it doesn't seem to flourish in the growing regions of Idaho or Oregon, it thrives in the drier climate of the Yakima Valley. Primarily used as a bittering hop, Yakima Cluster is not typically employed for imparting significant flavor or aroma to beers. However, when it does contribute to the flavor profile, it brings a moderate bittering quality combined with some earthy flavors. The aroma is flowery, with hints of sweet fruit. It's worth noting that all Cluster hops, including Yakima Cluster, are interchangeable in brewing, maintaining consistent quality. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Earthy, Floral, Fruity, Noble, Pepper, Spicy, Sweet, Woody

Name: Yakima Gold
Notes: A Washington State developed hop released in 2013. It has mild bittering and aroma properties and is a good dual-use hop. It decends from Early Cluster and a wild Slovenian male, so it has some Saaz like properties. Aromas include smooth hop flavor with a hint of lemon zest and grapefruit. Substitute: Saaz, Cluster, Cascade

Name: Yellow Sub
Notes: Yellow Sub is a relatively new hop blend released by BarthHaas Hop Creation. Yellow Sub is known for its distinct aroma and flavor profile, which is described as having a spicy, fruity, and citrusy character. The hop is often used in pale ales, IPAs, and other hop-forward beers. Yellow Sub hops have a relatively low alpha acid content, which means they are not particularly bitter. However, their high essential oil content makes them ideal for imparting flavor and aroma to beer. Brewers who use Yellow Sub often note its unique combination of spice and fruit flavors, with hints of lemon, lime, and orange. Overall, Yellow Sub is a versatile hop that can be used in a variety of beer styles, but is particularly well-suited for hop-forward beers where its aroma and flavor can shine. Flavor/Aroma: Apricot, Blackberry, Fruity, Orange, Sweet

Name: Zagrava
Notes: Zagrava hops, originating from Ukraine, represent a relatively new addition to the world of brewing, bringing with them a unique flavor profile that is both traditional and novel. The hop is known for its high essential oil content, which includes a significant amount of farnesene. This contributes to its aromatic properties, setting it apart from other varieties like Tradition and making it a closer relative to Spalter Select in terms of its sensory profile. The flavor and aroma profile of Zagrava is described as spicy-floral, with a strong herbal backbone complemented by fruit notes. This makes it an excellent choice for brewers looking to create beers with a rich bouquet and a nuanced taste. Its characteristics are well-suited for lagers, where the subtle interplay of flavors can shine through. The hop's origin in Ukraine, a region with a significant history of hop production, adds a layer of depth to its story and appeal. Ukrainian hops, such as Zagrava, are protected by national rights and have been registered at the EU Community Plant Variety Office (CPVO), ensuring their unique qualities are preserved and recognized. Aroma/Flavor: Citrus, Earthy, Floral, Fresh, Fruity, Herbal, Pine, Woody, Sweet

Name: Zamba (Samba)
Notes: Zamba, previously also known as Samba, is a unique hop blend that stands as the inaugural proprietary hop in the BSG hop program. This blend is exclusively available through the Brewers Supply Group. The name "Zamba" is inspired by the renowned Brazilian dance "Samba", reflecting the myriad of tropical flavors that this hop amalgamates. This connection to dance is not just in name; the hop's aroma is described as an "overwhelming tropical dance explosion", emphasizing its vibrant and lively character. Zamba is particularly suited for late hop additions and hop plugging, especially for Juicy and Hazy IPAs. However, its versatility doesn't end there; it can be incorporated into any hop-forward ale or lager. Sensory evaluations highlight its pronounced fruity character. The aroma and taste profile of Zamba is multifaceted, dominated by juicy tropical fruits such as pineapple and mango. This is complemented by notes of stone fruit, candy sweetness, and the citrusy zest of oranges and tangerines. Flavor/Aroma: Candy, Mango, Orange, Pineapple, Stone Fruit, Tangerine, Tropical Fruits

Name: Zeus
Notes: Super high alpha bittering hops developed in the Yakima Valley, WA Used for: Bittering, intense aroma Aroma: Aromatic, pleasant Substitutes: Other high alpha hops

Name: Zibi
Notes: Embark on a journey of innovation with Zibi, a novel hop variety destined to make waves in the craft beer world. Boasting exceptional potential, Zibi beckons brewers to explore its rich tropical tapestry, weaving together enchanting notes of mango, peach, and mirabelle plum. Flavor: Zibi emerges as a trailblazer, captivating the senses with an opulent tropical aroma. Dive into the luscious depths of mango, where the velvety sweetness of peach and the delicate allure of mirabelle plum create a symphony of flavors that define this exceptional hop. Aroma: Unlock the potential of Zibi as its intoxicating aroma unfolds. Picture the warmth of a tropical breeze, enriched with the irresistible scent of ripe mango, the succulence of peach, and the unique charm of mirabelle plum. Zibi is a promise of distinctive aromatics that elevate your brew to new heights.

Name: Zlatan
Notes: A Czech hop released in 1976 and derived from Saaz. Created in Zatac by the Hop Research Institute it attempts to maintain the character and aroma of Saaz. It has a more mild bitterness and noble aroma if used late, as a whirlpool or dry hop addition.

Name: Zoso
Notes: Originating from the United States, Zoso hops are exclusive to Colorado, specifically cultivated at the Misty Mountain Hop Farm nestled in the Colorado Rocky Mountains. These hops are characterized as dual-purpose, boasting a high alpha acid content of around 10%. The flavor profile of Zoso is reminiscent of the popular Centennial hops but packs a more pronounced citrus punch, making it a standout choice for those desiring a citrus-forward brew. These strong citrus notes can complement a variety of beer styles, especially those that benefit from a bright and zesty character. Flavor/Aroma: Citrus, Fresh, Zesty, Crisp

Name: Zumo
Notes: Zumo hops come from Segal Ranch down in the lower Yakima Valley. John Segal pioneered the commercial cultivation of the most iconic American hop: Cascade. He is also responsible for the recent cult favorite Anchovy hops. Now with the 2023 harvest, Zumo is already becoming a favorite amongst breweries such as Other Half and Russian River. Brewers who have already brewed with Zumo describe it as citrus, citrus, and more citrus. A prominent lime note makes it a perfect fit for Mexican Lagers, but it has also performed well in big IPAs. Try it for yourself today and brew up something amazing! Zumo Hops Brewing Values Aroma: Lime zest, orange, citrus. Yeah, like a lot of citrus... Alpha Acids: 5.5-6.5% Beta Acids: 4.0-5.0% Total Oils: 0.5-1.0 mL / 100g Recommended Styles: Mexican lagers, pale ales, hazies, IPAs

Name: Zythos
Notes: A proprietary pellet blend. Designed to complement existing IPA and Pale Ale hop varieties. Used for: American-style Ale Aroma: Distinct tropical (pineapple) and citrus tones, with slight pine characteristics Substitutions: Unknown Storage: Unknown 10-12.5% AA / 4.7-6.2% Beta
